items.title: Samsung S90H OLED TV Review
items.author: Bo Moore
items.content:
I've been reviewing TVs for the better part of five years now, and few lineups have impressed me as consistently as Samsung's S90-series. Having tested several generations of the OLED, it's become the TV I most often recommend to buyers who want flagship OLED performance and don't mind giving up Dolby Vision. Year after year, Samsung has found ways to squeeze more out of the mid-range panel without reinventing the formula, making the S90 line one of the safest bets in premium TVs, particularly for gamers.

The Samsung S90H OLED TV doesn't stray far from that winning blueprint, but that's hardly a criticism. Building on last year's model with a brighter, more refined panel, a 165Hz refresh rate borrowed from last year’s flagship , and all the gaming features you'd expect from a modern OLED, it's another incredibly capable all-rounder. The biggest hurdle this time is price. At $1,699 for the 55-inch model I tested, the S90H is a significant investment in a time when saving is utterly crucial. Fortunately, after spending several weeks with the set across movies, streaming, and gaming, I came away convinced the improvements are substantial enough to justify the premium.

Samsung S90H – Setup & Design

Samsung’s TV setup was always fairly simple, but it’s been streamlined into a total breeze. The dual feet simply slide into place and lock without the need for a screwdriver or any additional tools, making it one of the easiest TVs I’ve assembled in recent memory. You then simply pop on the pedestal part to the feet and it’s good to stand up on your entertainment system, but I would’ve appreciated a few more built-in cable management touches around the back.

Once powered on, Samsung’s SmartThings app handles most of the heavy lifting. Pairing the TV to Wi-Fi, signing into Samsung services, and connecting compatible devices takes just a few moments all on your smartphone, eliminating much of the usual setup friction. It’s the kind of process that gets you watching content instead of tapping through endless menus. I also love that you can upload and save your home page settings, so if you ever need to upgrade or already live inside the Samsung ecosystem, everything is right there for you on startup.

The 55-inch S90H model I tested is remarkably slim and surprisingly light at around 38 pounds (17.3 kg) with its stand attached, making it easy enough for one person to maneuver during setup. The chassis measures just under 1.6 inches thick at its deepest point, giving it an elegant profile whether it’s sitting on a media console or mounted to a wall.

Around the front, Samsung keeps distractions to a minimum. The bezels aren’t completely invisible, but they’re thin enough to disappear once content starts playing. The overall design feels understated rather than flashy. It lets the panel be the star of the show, which is exactly what an OLED should do, and even more impressive is that anti-glare matte finish that cuts through nasty reflections with ease.

Powering everything is Samsung’s NQ4 AI Gen3 Processor, which handles 4K AI Upscaling Pro, motion processing, and Samsung’s suite of AI picture enhancements. Navigation throughout Tizen feels as snappy as ever, apps launch without hesitation, and switching between HDMI inputs is quick. The Tizen OS has seen several improvements, which makes navigation so much simpler. More importantly, the processor never feels like it’s getting in the way – it keeps the interface responsive while leaving you free to decide how much of Samsung’s image processing you actually want enabled.

Samsung has also quietly improved Tizen over the past few generations. Gone is the older side-panel interface, replaced by a cleaner home screen that keeps apps, inputs, and settings organized across the top of the display. It still takes a little time to learn where everything lives, but once you do, navigation feels faster and far less cluttered than it used to. My only real annoyance is Samsung's optional Daily Board and Ambient features, which can greet you with weather updates and smart home information whenever you walk by the display. It's a clever idea, but one that can feel unnecessary if you're walking through the living room a lot.

I tested the 55-inch Samsung S90H OLED over the course of several days using a mix of movies, games, and calibration software. Alongside everyday viewing, I measured the TV using Datacolor’s SpyderPro in four configurations: Standard Mode (SDR), Filmmaker Mode (SDR), Standard Mode (HDR), and Filmmaker Mode (HDR). Testing focused on brightness, contrast, white point, gamma/EOTF tracking, color gamut coverage, and Delta-E color accuracy to compare Samsung’s default image processing against its creator-focused picture mode.

For real-world performance, I paired the TV with a Panasonic DP-UB820-K 4K Blu-ray player for physical media, a PlayStation 5 Pro, gaming PC, and Nintendo Switch 2, while also evaluating streaming content through Samsung’s built-in Tizen platform. Movies spanning modern HDR releases, classic DVDs, and streamed content helped evaluate everything from HDR presentation and OLED black levels to upscaling and motion handling.

The Samsung S90H OLED is a reminder that impressive hardware doesn't always translate into an impressive out-of-the-box experience. Beneath Samsung's processing sits an outstanding OLED panel capable of exceptional contrast, rich color, and plenty of brightness, but its default Eco and Standard picture modes are the biggest culprits behind the disappointing image quality many first-time buyers may experience.

If you simply unbox the TV and leave everything at its defaults, the S90H delivers the kind of image Samsung has become known for on showroom floors: bright, vivid, and immediately attention-grabbing. During testing, Standard Mode reached 411.8 nits in SDR, proving the S90H isn't shy with its luminance. The problem is that Samsung aggressively manipulates nearly every aspect of the image.

The most obvious example is white balance. As brightness increased, the measured white point climbed from 8,900K at the lowest setting to an icy 11,900K at full brightness. Since films, television, and games are typically mastered around the D65 reference point of roughly 6,500K, that shift introduces an increasingly cool, blue-tinted image – something Samsung users may know all too well. Whites lose their neutrality, skin tones become less lifelike, and scenes that should feel warm instead take on a colder appearance.

That's only part of the story. Standard Mode also alters gamma to brighten shadows and midtones beyond what content creators intended. While that makes dark scenes easier to see in a brightly lit room, it also strips away some of the depth that makes OLED displays such a compelling buy in the first place. Rather than letting perfect black levels create natural contrast, Samsung opts to reveal more of the image, flattening scenes that should feel moodier or more atmospheric. Samsung's motion smoothing, sharpening, and AI enhancements are also enabled by default, and while they're easily disabled, most owners won't venture into those settings on day one.

Those shortcomings became easy to spot once I started watching actual content. on 4K Ultra HD disc looked spectacular regardless of picture mode, but Standard Mode immediately pushed the film's iconic neon-soaked cityscapes further than they needed to go. Blues and cyans became noticeably cooler, while bright signage and reflections carried more intensity than I felt the source intended. It still looked undeniably impressive, but there was a subtle artificiality to the image in Standard Mode that became difficult to ignore once I switched over to Filmmaker. Likewise, intimate hearing room scenes exposed the same tendency with skin tones. Faces took on a slightly cooler complexion under Standard Mode, and while the image never looked outright bad, it lacked the natural warmth and nuance Christopher Nolan's cinematography is known for.

The calibration results reflected what I was seeing. Standard Mode produced an average Delta-E of 21.95 in SDR, indicating colors regularly deviated well beyond their intended values. HDR was even more aggressive, with an average Delta-E climbing to 24.65. In practical terms, the colors on-screen often become different shades entirely.

Thankfully, switching to Filmmaker Mode changes the conversation. Samsung managed to achieve excellent color accuracy without sacrificing brightness. In fact, Filmmaker Mode measured slightly brighter in SDR during testing, reaching 428.6 nits while simultaneously bringing the white point back to a much healthier 6,200K to 6,900K range. That gives the image a far more natural appearance. Skin tones regain warmth, whites stop looking artificially cool, and the picture no longer feels like it's trying to impress you at every opportunity. Instead, it simply lets the OLED panel and director’s intent speak for themselves.

Filmmaker Mode isn't entirely flawless, however. Samsung still trends ever so slightly cooler than the D65 reference, though it's minor enough that most viewers are unlikely to notice. More apparent is the tradeoff introduced by the TV's excellent anti-glare coating. It does an excellent job of diffusing reflections and makes daytime viewing far more comfortable than many glossy OLEDs, but it also introduces a subtle softness to the image. Side-by-side with a glossy panel, the S90H doesn't quite have the same razor-sharp appearance, though it's a compromise I was more than willing to make given how great the TV looks even in my bright living room.

That restraint became especially apparent during opening flyover of New Port City. Against the OLED's perfectly black night sky, neon advertisements remained vibrant without bleeding into one another, while subtle gradients in the darkness stayed intact instead of being overwhelmed by exaggerated highlights. Even the film's darker sequences, like Major's thermoptic camouflage and the museum confrontation with the Puppet Master, retained remarkable shadow detail without sacrificing the depth OLED displays are famous for. proved equally revealing in a completely different way. The film's close-up dialogue scenes highlighted just how much more lifelike skin tones became in Filmmaker Mode, while the black-and-white sequences maintained clean grayscale transitions with excellent contrast and no obvious clipping in brighter portions of the frame.

HDR follows a similar pattern. Standard Mode unlocks more of the panel's raw color volume, measuring 93% DCI-P3 coverage compared to Filmmaker Mode's measured 75%. On paper, that sounds like an easy win for Samsung's default preset, but color volume only matters if those colors are being reproduced accurately. on Prime Video illustrated that difference particularly well in its vibrant reds and greens against the ink-black backdrop of space. Brilliant stars, spacecraft lighting, and reflective metallic surfaces all carried plenty of impact in both modes, but Filmmaker Mode rendered them with noticeably more restraint, avoiding the exaggerated, almost showroom-like appearance Standard Mode occasionally introduced. The result was a more convincing image, although the limitations of a compressed streaming source were still apparent in some darker gradients. The S90H does an admirable job minimizing those shortcomings, but it can't invent detail that simply isn't there.

Perhaps the biggest surprise, though, was the S90H's upscaling. Watching Ridley Scott's on DVD could have easily exposed the weaknesses of a modern 4K display, yet Samsung's processor handled the aging source with confidence. Compression artifacts weren't scrubbed away with heavy-handed noise reduction, film grain remained largely intact, and Osaka's rain-soaked streets retained far more texture than I expected from a standard-definition disc. There were still moments where compression noise and ringing peeked through – as they inevitably will with an older DVD – but Samsung wisely avoids overcorrecting. It's the kind of performance that makes revisiting an older movie collection far more enjoyable on a 55-inch 4K OLED.

Just as importantly, gamma tracking closely followed the BT.1886 reference curve, preserving shadow detail without artificially brightening it. OLED panels don't need help producing convincing contrast, and Filmmaker Mode wisely steps back and lets the hardware's perfect blacks create the depth naturally. Motion handling is equally impressive once Samsung's default smoothing is dialed back. Films retain their intended cinematic cadence without introducing distracting soap opera effects, though extremely slow camera pans can still exhibit the faint stutter that's characteristic of OLED's near-instantaneous response times.

Filmmaker Mode proved that point clearly. HDR color accuracy improved dramatically, with average Delta-E dropping from 24.65 to just 3.75 while the display tracked the HDR ST.2084 EOTF far more closely. The result is an image that looks less exaggerated but significantly more believable, with smoother gradients, more convincing highlights, and colors that appear faithful to the source rather than enhanced for effect.

It's also worth noting that HDR brightness measurements through SpyderPro should be viewed in context. Consumer OLEDs employ automatic brightness limiting, and HDR testing through Windows introduces additional variables that consumer calibration software isn't specifically designed to overcome. While those limitations can affect absolute HDR brightness readings, they don't change the broader takeaway from the testing: Samsung's processing has a much larger impact on picture quality than the panel itself.

That's ultimately what defines the S90H OLED TV. Samsung has once again built an OLED that hides its best qualities behind the wrong default settings. Spend thirty seconds switching into Filmmaker Mode and cutting out some of the AI-assisted features, and you're rewarded with one of the most balanced OLED images in its class, delivering rich HDR, excellent SDR performance, and some of the best out-of-the-box accuracy I've measured from Samsung.

Samsung has spent years building some of the best gaming TVs on the market, and the S90H OLED TV continues that tradition. Equipped with four HDMI 2.1 inputs that feature Variable Refresh Rate (VRR), Auto Low Latency Mode (ALLM), and a maximum refresh rate of 165Hz – a beefier number first introduced on last year's flagship Samsung S95F – the S90H feels purpose-built for gamers who want to squeeze every ounce of performance from their hardware. That said, Samsung still hasn’t copied the USB-C video output found on the Hisense U8 Mini-LED TV, a feature I’d love to see on more displays. Plus, there’s still no HDMI 2.2 input, but hopefully TVs will start to pick that up by 2027.

On , everything I threw at the S90H looked superb. showcased deep contrast during nighttime drives through Los Santos, while HDR highlights from headlights, streetlights, and sunsets popped against the OLED's perfect black levels without looking overprocessed. benefited from the panel's strong shadow detail, preserving environmental textures inside dimly lit vaults and abandoned buildings, while delivered smooth camera pans and crisp player animations throughout fast-paced matches. Motion remained clean across the board, and I never noticed distracting ghosting or blur during extended play sessions.

PC gaming proved equally impressive, thanks largely to the fact that the S90H is equipped with both AMD FreeSync and Nvidia G-Sync compatibility. Running Assassin's Creed Black Flag Resynced and in 4K highlighted just how striking Samsung's OLED panel can be when paired with modern HDR effects, but it was that really demonstrated the benefit of the S90H's 165Hz refresh rate. Fast-paced mech combat remained exceptionally responsive, with fluid camera movement and razor-sharp motion that made high frames feel genuinely worthwhile. Whether navigating menus or diving into chaotic battles, the TV always felt quick to respond.

Even the looked fantastic in 4K despite its more modest hardware. benefited from the OLED panel's rich colors, while looked vibrant without becoming oversaturated. It's a reminder that the S90H can elevate practically every platform you connect to it, even without the quantum dot filter on its more expensive flagship model.

If gaming is your primary focus, I'd have no hesitation recommending the S90H over many of its OLED rivals. Samsung's continued omission of Dolby Vision might sound like a disadvantage on paper, but it's largely irrelevant for PlayStation 5 Pro and Switch 2 owners, as those consoles don’t support Dolby Vision gaming anyway. That means you're getting outstanding HDR performance, a higher refresh rate than many competing OLEDs, excellent gaming features, and one of the most responsive gaming experiences available today without giving up much.

It's funny how a TV's quality is often best judged by people who don't spend their days reviewing them. My daily driver is still a projector, but after a few weeks with the S90H OLED, one of my friends stopped mid-conversation to ask what TV I was using because it “looked incredible." That's the power of Samsung's OLEDs. They don't just disappear into the background, but have a way of making people notice them, even when the screen’s turned off.

If you're shopping for a premium OLED TV and gaming is anywhere near the top of your priority list, the Samsung S90H is an easy recommendation. Its excellent panel, outstanding gaming feature set, and dramatically improved image accuracy in Filmmaker Mode make it one of the most well-rounded TVs Samsung has produced. It isn't a set that demands hours of tweaking or professional calibration to look its finest, simply switching away from the default Eco and Standard picture modes unlocks an image that's bright, cinematic, and impressively faithful to the source.

The biggest consideration is price. At $1,699 for the 55-inch model, the S90H OLED asks for a considerable investment, and that alone may push some buyers toward older models or competing OLEDs that frequently see aggressive discounts, like the LG C-series. At the time of writing, the , the S90H’s closest competitor, sits as low as $1,499 on Amazon. While it’s not quite the same, offering just 120Hz and no matte anti-glare coating, it’s always been the best value option for most buyers.

Even so, after living with the S90H TV for several weeks, I came away feeling that Samsung has made meaningful improvements where they matter most. The brighter panel, 165Hz refresh rate, refined processing, and exceptional gaming performance all add up to a TV that's easy to recommend, and one that continues the S90 series' reputation as my go-to OLED for most buyers willing to overlook the lack of Dolby Vision.

items.title: Samsung Galaxy Z Flip 8 Review So Far
items.author: Jacqueline Thomas
items.content:
Samsung has been making its foldables for eight generations now, and while the company has tacked on the ‘Ultra’ branding to the Fold 8, the Flip 8 isn’t all that different to its predecessor. The main difference this time around is that it’s using a more powerful processor, and the price has gone up by $100.

The Samsung Galaxy Z Flip 8 is packed with the same Qualcomm Snapdragon 8 Elite Gen 5 chip that’s powering both the Galaxy Z Fold 8 Ultra and the S26 Ultra. I haven’t had a chance to really put this phone through its paces yet, but just swapping to this high-end SoC, rather than the MediaTek chip in last generation’s model, should make the Flip 8 a much better gaming phone than last year’s version.

If you’ve ever seen any iteration of the Samsung Galaxy Z Flip, the Flip 8 will look incredibly familiar. When the phone is closed, you can interact with a 4.1-inch display that’ll let you run most, but not all apps. You can then open it up to a full 6.9-inch display, albeit with a bit of a narrower aspect ratio than most phones.

Out of all the foldables I’ve used, this type of form factor has always been the most novel to me. There’s just something about being able to fold a phone up into a little square that fits into whichever pocket or purse I’m using that day. It does help that Samsung’s Flip phones have been getting thinner every year too.

The Flip 8 is no different, shrinking down to 6.1mm when unfolded, which is down from the Flip 7’s 6.5mm. That’s not a huge difference, and you probably wouldn’t even notice the difference when the phones are next to each other, but it does let Samsung call it the ‘thinnest Flip yet,’ I suppose.

Both displays are AMOLED panels, with the outer display having a resolution of 1048 x 948, along with a 2520 x 1080 inner display. The pixel density of 391 ppi isn’t by modern smartphone standards, especially considering that the $799 Moto Razr has an incredibly similar display, albeit with a weaker chipset.

Like with any foldable, there is a crease in the middle of the inner display, but it is slightly less noticeable than it was on the Flip 7. I do notice it when I hold it at a certain angle, but in general with these foldables, it’s something my brain basically erases from my attention after a little while. The bigger problem on older foldables and, uh, flippables, was a gap between the edge of the crease and the rest of the chassis, which would allow dust to get in. Thankfully that’s not a problem anymore.

And just like with the Galaxy Z Fold 8 Ultra, Samsung has added a new titanium alloy film and a titanium plate underneath the display, which should help prevent any damage from unfolding and re-folding the display, as well as make bending it backwards incredibly difficult. I didn’t actually test whether or not I could snap it in half, of course, but it should be pretty durable.

The Samsung Galaxy Z Flip 8 has basically the same camera as the Z Flip 7, but it does include 2x optical zoom, where the 7 relied entirely on digital zoom. That was the biggest problem with last year’s flip, as the software-based ‘AI zoom’ wasn’t great at retaining detail.

I haven’t had the chance to go out and put the Z Flip 8’s camera through its paces yet, but Samsung is claiming that all of its foldables this year have much better night-time performance as well. However, I’ve never seen a smartphone come out that claimed to be good at night photography, so we’ll just have to wait and see.

For the longest time, the Samsung Galaxy Z Flip has always been a bit slower than the Fold, mostly due to size constraints. However, Samsung has found a way to shove the Snapdragon 8 Elite Gen 5 and 12GB of RAM into the Z Flip 8, so it should be able to hold its own against other smartphones. And, it really needs to hold its own, because at $1,199, it’s charging flagship money.

I’ve only had a few hours with the Samsung Galaxy Z Flip 8 so far, so I can’t say definitively how well it stacks up against the other phones I’ve tested with this chipset. But, it’s likely that it will lag at least a little bit behind something like the Galaxy S26 Ultra or the OnePlus 11, thanks to the heat constraints that come with a thinner chassis that’s essentially split in half.

I did play a bit of Arknights: Endfield on the Flip 8, and it performed just as well as the Fold 8 Ultra did. There is a caveat there, though: because of the lower display resolution, the game is quite a bit easier to run. Endfield tosses up a big, scary warning whenever you try to change the frame rate from 45 fps to 60 fps, but because the Flip 8 only has a 1080p display, it should be able to handle this higher setting, but it will get quite toasty.

Even when you’re not running experimental settings in games, the Flip 8 has some heating issues. In any game where you have to hold the phone horizontally, the outer display gets quite warm to the touch. It’s not hot enough to burn you or anything, but it does get uncomfortable if you’re holding it a while. On top of that, it just feels weird to have half the phone heating up, while the other side is relatively cool.

You probably won’t get much longer gaming sessions out of the Z Flip 8, anyway, though. This year’s model packs the same 4300mAh battery as its predecessor. That’s not the end of the world, after all there’s only so much battery you can fit in such a small phone, but it is a disappointment, especially considering that Samsung was able to upgrade the Fold 8 Ultra’s battery.

I need to spend a bit more time with the Galaxy Z Flip 8 before I can say anything for sure, but I love that Samsung packed it with a more powerful chip this time around. However, with the added power comes added heat, and I’ll have to see whether or not the Galaxy Z Flip 8 actually keeps performance churning over long workloads. That’s something that Samsung’s thinner phones have been struggling with for a while now, but if the throttling problem has been solved, the Galaxy Z Flip 8 could be a great gaming phone for anyone that doesn’t want a thick slab in their pocket all the time.

Jackie Thomas is the Hardware and Buying Guides Editor at IGN and the PC components queen. You can follow her

items.title: Motor City Review
items.author: Arnold T. Blumberg
items.content:
will be released in theaters on July 24.

There are fewer than a dozen lines of audible, functional dialogue in Motor City — Potsy Ponciroli’s ’70s-set Venice Film Festival premiere — resulting in a gruff, stripped-bare action film that highlights the genre’s worst and best. That it comes down on the side of “highly entertaining” is a minor miracle, if only because Ponciroli and his cast are completely locked in to the moods and movements that dominate the screen, allowing for an unfiltered (if musically overbearing) piece with its own brutal rhythms.

Granted, when you sacrifice words in a movie that’s this reliant on minor details, you end up either focusing on bits of paper, like modern silent intertitles — foreclosure notices, job applications, news articles, and so on — or you end up forgoing information entirely. This applies to Motor City’s protagonist, John Miller, a hulking former soldier with a nondescript job and close crew-cut. However, when you have an actor like Alan Ritchson in the part (think Schwarzenegger with more sensitivity), it’s a sacrifice you can afford. The movie opens with Miller wreaking vengeful havoc, dropping a body on a speeding car from a rooftop before firing a shotgun while getting flung through the air.

It’s a blast, no pun intended, and it builds just enough curiosity for you to get with the pseudo-silent program when Motor City flashes back to several months prior. Miller, a lion-maned hustler on probation — again, those are all the details we have — proposes to his waitress girlfriend Sophie (Shailene Woodley) before being framed in a major drug bust. Through flashbacks and silent stares, we learn that the mastermind behind his false imprisonment is slimy drug lord Reynolds (Ben Foster), who has numerous corrupt cops at his disposal, like the leather trenchcoat-sporting Savick (Pablo Schreiber). Meanwhile, more clean-cut detectives like Ben McKenzie’s Kent seem intent on unraveling this conspiracy.

It’s in establishing these characters and their motives that familiar stock types and obvious costume design come in handy. Reynolds especially benefits from this, as well as from Foster’s casting as a skeevy, potbellied, envious ex, whose ploy is all about winning Sophie back. In the meantime, Miller languishes in prison, his hair and beard growing ever longer, and prepares to escape and exact revenge with the help of two former comrades on the outside: Youngblood (Lionel Boyce) and Singh (Amar Chadha-Patel).

The trio, who eventually stage a blazing clockwork prison escape, are pretty cool, albeit in familiar ways, what with their straight-from-a-soup-can swagger with which they strut away from explosions. However, this being a bog-standard revenge flick in every way (except its sound), you already know which characters are more disposable than others, which is to say “anyone who isn’t a white man.”

Still, Motor City makes for an engaging experience, if only because it forces its author and audience alike into a somewhat non-traditional mode of filmmaking and viewing. It shouldn’t be this out of the ordinary for an American action flick to depend on visual cues, but in an increasingly homogenized action landscape, watching body language, blocking, and editing tell a story is a delight unto itself. It certainly helps that the movie taps into visceral sensations along the way, such as its vicious bloodshed and one particularly exciting no-holds-barred combat scene in the confines of an elevator. All of this is enhanced by Ritchson and Foster delivering fully-committed performances as admittedly retrograde archetypes in pursuit of their blood feud over a woman.

Ritchson and Foster deliver fully-committed performances as admittedly retrograde archetypes in pursuit of their blood feud over a woman. 

If there’s one place Motor City drops the ball, however, it’s in its music selection. Any film that muffles and circumvents the spoken word is bound to rely on other auditory means, and while its ambient sounds are just fine — they’re functional and unremarkable rather than absorbing — the movie’s wall-to-wall needle drops in the vein of the Guardians of the Galaxy movies feel a touch random. The artists in question are certainly era-appropriate — David Bowie, Donna Summer, Fleetwood Mac, and so on — and they prevent boredom during the initial setup before the movie finally lets Steve Jablonsky’s booming score do its work. But the title is Motor City, and there’s nothing particularly Detroit-esque about it, least of all its tracklist: The Bowie song in question isn’t even “Panic in Detroit!” This was the era of Alice Cooper and Iggy Pop, and the birth of techno; you couldn’t ask for a more appropriately propulsive in-built soundtrack, and the movie doesn’t take advantage of any of it.

Ultimately, this ends up indicative of how even Motor City ends up part of the aforementioned homogenized traditions of American action films, where time, place, and music have an increasing unimportance. You can transpose the story to any other setting or era without losing all that much, which is disappointing. But regardless of its missteps, that the movie works at all is almost worth applauding, even if only for the fact that Ponciroli gets enough audio-visual basics right to craft a coherent film of wanton violence. It’s pretty silly and pretty fun.

items.title: Halo: Campaign Evolved Review
items.author: Michael Higham
items.content:
You can tell a single-player campaign is truly great when revisiting it sparks a similar joy as your very first run, even when you remember how it all plays out beat-by-beat. Pistol shots on scrambling Grunts, sticking Elites with plasma grenades, popping Flood like bubble wrap with an Assault Rifle; Halo: Campaign Evolved rekindled the familiar feeling of playing the original on a rented Xbox at my grandma's house in 2001. But as much as I adore Halo's campaign, with its simple yet thoughtful combat design, you can only play the hits so often. When the base game, the Anniversary Edition, and the Master Chief Collection are all readily available in 2026, it's easy to see this remake as more of an Unreal Engine test than anything. There’s a new batch of Skulls and three prequel missions to sweeten the deal, which are a fun few hours to blast through – but the changes outside the graphical overhaul are otherwise subtle, and as a whole, neither definitively improve nor detract from what I played 25 years ago. So, while it's great to see how well Halo has aged, we've been down this road many times before.

There aren't really any surprises here for those who've played Halo before; Master Chief awakens as the UNSC flagship is invaded by the alien Covenant and he escapes by landing on a mysterious ring-shaped planet. In its twists and turns through various biomes and chromed-out Forerunner halls, you uncover the truth about Halo itself, paving the way for the rest of the series. It's a clean, no-nonsense story that's refreshingly simple in retrospect. And let's not forget Master Chief, alongside Cortana, embodied the sentiment that , especially when you're going full speed in a Warthog as the classic theme song starts kicking in.

Campaign Evolved includes some brand-new cutscenes that teeter between kind of hokey to just . Pulling up to the beachfront in a Pelican for The Silent Cartographer has a little extra gravity but I do miss that original first-person fly-in, and the scene at the bow of the Pillar of Autumn for the opening mission kind of kills a sense of urgency. While these are efforts in being more cinematic and detailed with the story, they're sometimes missing the natural flow of they replaced.

Halo's combat calculus remains solid all these years later.

Overall, there's beauty in how quick Halo is to the punch, and it's this initial setup of a distinct sci-fi world that set the stage for some of the best designed shootouts of its era, which revolutionized first-person games on console at the time. The sense of wonder when trying to figure out which direction to go upon exploring Halo itself as Covenant ships constantly drop Grunts, Elites, and Jackals made for encounters that felt organic and varied. That mix of enemies alone was strong enough bread-and-butter for basic encounters, and when they're accompanied by armor-clad Hunters or cloaked Elites lurking with energy swords, I'm reminded that Halo was so much more than a scripted shooting gallery. And it's still a hearty challenge on Legendary runs, rivaling FPS campaigns of the modern day. As annoying as it may be to wipe from something like a pesky suicidal Grunt running around with two plasma grenades in-hand, you just have to accept it and play a little smarter next time. Positioning, grenade placement, and making the most of whatever weapons are available are part of why Halo's combat calculus remains solid all these years later.

Certain stretches do rely on repeat corridors for a little too long. Working your way through The Library alongside Guilty Spark, grinding through Covenant ship hallways, or backtracking during Two Betrayals; these aren't the most exciting parts of Halo. However, the fundamentals of its iconic, satisfying gunplay still make the repetition enjoyable at least. Moments like being able to take a Banshee or Scorpion tank through snow-covered valleys and approach open firefights with Halo's sense of freedom make the lulls a little more forgivable.

The Unreal Engine 5 makeover is impressive in the way that high-fidelity games built on Unreal often have an approximation of photorealism. Those finer details are definitely noticeable, which is all the more apparent since I replayed this campaign in the Master Chief Collection just before this review. Fidelity alone isn't really what made Halo enduring, though, and the change in atmosphere is also a change in personality. It's like every surface has a glossy sheen to it, as if it was weirdly buffed out with gallons of Brasso. Now, I remember the same debate when the Anniversary Edition came along, but at least it did have the instant visual swap button and of course, Campaign Evolved doesn't since it's a rebuilt game. The change in aesthetic doesn't hurt the experience, and while it's nice to see the modernization, it isn’t always a stylistic improvement necessarily.

There's this odd fuzziness in the image quality I notice in some UE5 games that's present here, almost like a weird film grain you can't turn off, and some weird distortion around weapons models when you're in motion. That said, there are moments where the visual overhaul had me taken aback. The way the setting sun beamed through the cracks on the Pillar of Autumn in The Maw fed into the bleak, melancholic feeling of the final mission. The way plasma weapons lit up cavern walls as the Covenant and Flood duked it out added some depth to the tight spaces and chaotic battles therein. Along with the mood lighting and slimy biomechanical motifs of later missions, it reinforced the idea that the original Halo could have a slight horror edge to it at times.

Campaign Evolved also sort of Frankensteins parts of other Halo games together. The physics and controls seem to be based largely off of Halo: Reach, while character models are borrowed from a mishmash of others. Those are some of the more subtle examples, but the inclusion of several weapons that weren't usable in the original is a much more noticeable one. The purist in me wasn't always a fan of this move, but when I was launching Fuel Rods into enemies, lunging with Energy Swords, or plucking out Flood with a Sentinel Beam, I really couldn't complain since they feel incredible to use and add variety to combat.

I feel similarly about the addition of sprinting, which changes the cadence of the campaign in key parts and makes long stretches of corridors easier to blast through (and let me run past encounters I'd rather not engage in altogether). Medkits have been scrapped in favor of regenerative health, the Rocket Launcher can lock onto vehicles, and you can also keep equipment tucked away to activate when you want – these aren't huge tweaks per se, but I, for one, value being able to hit the Overshield just before pulling up on two Hunters at the same time.

While it's nice to see the modernization, it isn’t always a stylistic improvement necessarily.

I'd be remiss not to mention Skulls, the secret little modifiers you'll find in the most unlikely places in each mission. This is a carryover from the Anniversary Edition, with a batch of new ones added on top. To me, Skulls have been one of the more interesting ways a campaign adds replay value in both having to hunt for them and then redo missions with them active. Runs can get pretty bizarre with goofy changes like Grunt Birthday Party or challenging ones where you can only charge your shield through melee kills. The Remix modifiers are a welcome addition, where you can either randomize the types of enemies you encounter or weapons you pick up throughout the campaign. I like how off-the-rails these things can be, and in the absence of PvP multiplayer, these can liven up a co-op playthrough with absolute chaos – mostly the good kind.

The big attraction, however, are the three new prequel missions that take place a year before the events of the main campaign. Master Chief and Sergeant Johnson tag-team rescue operation to save UNSC Marines held captive on a Covenant ship, and while they're not exactly revolutionary levels, they fit the mold of a traditional Halo campaign in terms of encounter design and challenge. That also means a chunk of time is, again, spent on bobbing and weaving through ship hallways as you mow down the usual suspects.

A handful of moments do shine as bright as the new blinding flash of a plasma grenade in UE5. The gilded halls of the ship's high-class rooms give a retrofuturist aesthetic akin to Destiny's Leviathan raid, and the outdoor combat arenas have a clever way of working into the story of these prequel missions while providing a little more room for those open encounters Halo has historically been good at.

The lore implications add a little extra depth to our understanding of the Covenant, even if they don't move the needle in terms of what's established in the existing Halo story. The storytelling itself leans into more cheesy action movie tropes, but I'm not one who's precious about that part of the series. And if it means more time with Chief and Johnson together, I'm not going to turn my nose up at it.

I appreciate the roughly two hours the bonus missions add given that it's more Halo.

Fights include your typical wave of Covenant foes, but the new Brutes present a fun little twist to combat encounters. They have a glowing chip on their chest and will go berserk and attack anything in sight if you shoot it out, which can be an asset in tougher battles. It's also fun to plink the chip and just watch them go to town on defenseless Grunts. As imposing as they may be when they're rushing you down, keeping the overpowered Needle Rifle on hand made short work of them, or any enemy for that matter. I was also hyped for the dogfights that were showcased in the leadup to release, and while it was a neat little diversion to take a Seraph into the stars for actual space combat, it was short-lived and wrapped up just as it was starting to take off.

I appreciate the roughly two hours the bonus missions add given that it's more Halo (and technically new) Halo, and I'm glad co-op is seamlessly woven into the experience. But when a good chunk of Halo's legacy lies in PvP multiplayer (and perhaps a bit with PvE Firefights), I can't help but feel something important is missing in a campaign-only remake. So, who is this for? Well, it's for Halo Studios; a gesture of good will that it has figured out the technical path forward for a franchise that's been underserved in recent years and one that's expected to be part of Xbox's increasing reliance on revitalizing legacy franchises. With that in mind, Campaign Evolved proves that while the original Halo didn't really need a remake, this at least still has some spark left in it with the hope is that it leads to fresher ideas.

items.title: Star Trek: Strange New Worlds Season 4, Episode 1 Review - Age of the Dinosaurs
items.author: Scott Collura
items.content:
But also… whoa. The USS Enterprise crew is responsible for… the creation of the human race!?

After the rash of high-concept Strange New Worlds episodes in recent seasons – musicals, animated hijinks, retro TV space adventures, etc. – getting a good old-fashioned adventure-of-the-week story here to kick off Season 4 is refreshing. It’s not trying to be anything other than what it is – a fun, cool story for Captain Pike and the crew that also happens to lean into the mind-blowing “can you believe they went there?” zone.

Things kick off with Pike (Anson Mount), La'an (Christina Chong) and Dr. M'Benga (Babs Olusanmokun) on horseback on an alien world, where they’ve just dropped some medicine that will apparently save the planet’s population. Pike’s in his groove here – he’s got his own saddle, you know! – but after six months straight of intense missions, back on the ship Una (Rebecca Romijn) asks him if it isn’t time to slow down?

Pike is of course still mourning the loss of Captain Marie Batel, his lover who was lost to the vagaries of the space-time continuum at the end of Season 3. Dude’s had it tough; he also has weighing on him constantly, even if he doesn’t talk about it much these days. He’s losing himself in his work to escape the reality of these things.

So when Admiral April (Adrian Holmes) shows up with not just a new, chill mission – go check out this rad interstellar storm, hang out, and take some readings – but also a new job offer – a promotion to commodore – Pike will finally have some time to sit back and think. Or so he believes, if not for the whole getting thrown back in time to the age of the dinosaurs thing.

It’s Burrows' interplay with Mount that carries the show as they debate the very Federation concept of versus the very Martian ethos of .

While Una, La’an and Ortegas’ (Melissa Navia) mission to what turns out to be prehistoric Earth is enjoyable, and certainly the more flashy “show it off in the trailer” of the two main plot threads here, it’s not always convincing from a visual or dramatic perspective. Strange New Worlds’ reliance on the allows it to visit exactly that – strange new worlds – but that doesn’t mean the experience is always believable. Plus, Una versus a T. rex probably would’ve played better as scary rather than funny, which is how it’s handled here. These guys are being very casual considering their situation! (Star Trek vs. dinosaurs should’ve just gotten its own episode.)

No, the A storyline back on the ship is where the money’s at in “Valles Marineris.” While the dinosaur adventure is happening down below, the disabled Enterprise is captured by an unfamiliar alien race who turn out to be warlike Martians! Saffron Burrows (Wing Commander, Deep Blue Sea) represents these folks as the hardened Battle Commander Cassel, and it’s her interplay with Mount’s Pike that carries the show as they debate the very Federation concept of versus the very Martian ethos of . As Spock might say, it’s quite… stimulating.

(Meanwhile, a conversation between Ortegas and La’an down on the planet drives home this debate. Ortegas takes the compassionate side, while La’an is pretty stone cold. But her increasingly aggressive posture is touched on again during the episode’s postscript, where we learn that she’s worried that her genetically enhanced nature may be getting the better of her lately. Khaaaaaaan!!!)

Burrows is the kind of high-quality guest star who can really elevate an episode of Star Trek, and her steely, emotionally restrained turn as Cassel – who is clearly devastated by the horrors her world has endured in its war with the insectoid species the Dol’Drm – mixes well with Pike’s aw-shucks can’t-we-all-just-get-along-isms. When Pike happens to say the word “alone,” the universal translator presents it to Cassel as “widower” because of her species’ many different interpretations of the word based on the speaker’s inflection. She offers her condolences to Pike, who after all, essentially a widower now. He certainly feels that way, it seems.

That this all climaxes in a clutch moment for the captain where he must, on a hunch, decide to allow Cassel to destroy the entire planet of the Dol’Drm – while also sacrificing herself – is pretty ballsy. Of course, being one of Starfleet’s finest, he was right to follow his gut, and this entire caper has been one big time loop. The Enterprise had to come back in time in order to trigger the events that would cause the destruction of the Dol’Drm, which ironically enough will lead to the death of Mars as a habitable world as well as the dawning of a new age on Earth where humanity will rise. Awesome.

But perhaps where this story thread stumbles is in Pike’s easy acceptance of his decision. He did, after all, enable the genocide of an entire species – time loop be damned – and while we’re supposed to believe that this adventure has helped him come to terms with the notion that he needs to live in the here and now rather than continuously rushing off to avoid his problems, I’m not sure the script quite sells that. “I want to focus on my now, and that’s here, with Enterprise,” he tells the Doc. So he’s offer. This time.

Questions and Notes from the Q Continuum:

  • Pike and his saddle… big Picard energy going on there.
  • A 15-minute long cold open before the title credits is… a lot.
  • The crew has just done six months of missions, but aren’t they already on a five-year mission?
  • Another Doctor Who reference. That’ll do at this point, Pelia, that’ll do.
  • “Tract-OR beam.” Is Ethan Peck putting his own spin on Leonard Nimoy’s famous “sen-SORE” pronunciation of “sensor”?
  • So the phasers have a new disintegration setting… but Ortegas insists on calling it the “vaporization thingee” just to annoy Scotty (who isn’t even there!).
  • Wait, Una wasn’t already a commander?
  • “Sometimes it’s just recipes.”

items.title: Pinocchio Unstrung Review
items.author: Arnold T. Blumberg
items.content:
Just when I thought the Twisted Childhood Universe — aka the Poohniverse — couldn't get any worse, it didn't. It got better… exponentially better in fact.

In 2023, filmmakers Rhys Frake-Waterfield and Scott Chambers kickstarted what would become their TCU with the quite rotten Winnie-the-Pooh: Blood and Honey. Since then, they’ve overseen horror spins on , , and Pooh’s Hundred Acre horde to varying results (Winnie-the-Pooh: Blood and Honey 2 is a Terrifier-inspired hoot). But there’s hope yet for these lower-budget public domain horror adaptations; Pinocchio Unstrung is a legitimately funny and brutally bloody take on Carlo Collodi's 1883 novel, The Adventures of Pinocchio, and while short of perfection, it’s easily the TCU’s crowning release to date.

Frake-Waterfield writes and directs a nightmarish comedy about Pinocchio (voiced by Jude-Evan Lloyd), a living wooden figure who wants to become a real boy. In this scenario, he’s brought to life by Geppetto (Richard Brake) as a forever companion for his grandson, James (Cameron Bell), after enduring the loss of his parents as well as his best mate. Pinocchio is immediately protective of James; he learns that an alpha always protects the herd while watching wildlife documentaries. With the aid of a nefarious cricket (voiced by Robert Englund), Pinocchio starts punishing anyone who harms James — dentists, drunk drivers, prep school bullies— while turning himself into a real boy with flesh, organs, and a newfound family.

Frake-Waterfield is channeling the Child’s Play universe here, specifically Seed of Chucky’s goofier bits and SYFY’s gonzo Chucky television show. It’s whimsical, silly, and vicious out of naivety, with a supernatural outcast seeking compassion and acceptance amidst grief and pain. There’s an enchanting connection to nature in Geppetto’s craftsmanship — what’s born from soil and grows into trees — but also a putrid darkness about his methods. Funnily enough, The Adventures of Pinocchio doesn’t shy away from terrifying imagery with pages that feel torn from horror authors, yet Frake-Waterfield finds fresh avenues to inject genre flourishes. And surprisingly, he nails the corrupted vibe of a twisted fairy tale, with Geppetto serving as a Dr. Frankenstein with a green thumb.

Frake-Waterfield nails the corrupted vibe of a twisted fairy tale, with Geppetto serving as a Dr. Frankenstein with a green thumb.

Pinocchio Unstrung boasts an old-school genre personality thanks to Todd Masters and MastersFX, who built a fully functional Pinocchio puppet; shades of Guillermo del Toro’s interpretation, but eerily inhuman. He looks fab, down to feathered bark that mimics hair and sunken eyes for a solid creep factor. Frake-Waterfield’s team of puppeteers gives Pinocchio eerie life paired with lil’ Lloyd’s prepubescent voice acting. There’s wonder behind Pinocchio’s words, brimming with curiosity, and a sweetness behind his jumbled motivations. Yes, he might be bludgeoning a catty schoolgirl with a sledgehammer, but Lloyd’s delivery juxtaposes the softness of a youngling we want to protect. It’s never as simple as “Pinocchio bad, people good,” which yields more thematic warmth than expected and coddles Pinocchio with an empathetic touch.

However, the script’s folkloric invocations don’t stop Pinocchio Unstrung from being a face-clawing, spine-snapping, appendage-removing gorefest. The first kill brought a howl out of me like a cartoon character, and the slaughtering only gets more gruesome from there. Gym equipment, toolshed objects, and Pinocchio’s own pointy fingers are all implements of death, as bodies are mangled beyond recognition. Frake-Waterfield leans heavily on cheeky references to everything from The Texas Chain Saw Massacre to Jaws, but never as a distraction. Pinocchio’s murder spree recalls the brutality of Jay Baruchel’s Random Acts of Violence, where dead bodies become a psycho’s art pieces, while still keeping that bouncy Chucky-esque energy. Just when you think Frake-Waterfield won’t push another boundary, his effects team tests their limits by snapping a meathead in half or ripping someone’s scalp off like a tortured Barbie doll.

Kudos to Richard Brake for anchoring the film’s seediest bites, since — let’s be real — he’s never played squeaky clean. You know Geppetto’s arc from the minute Brake smiles at James, despite his promises of friendship and pizza. As Pinocchio Unstrung spirals into a descent of hacked-up corpses and destroyed childhoods, Brake is there to hoist an underlying nastiness into the spotlight. He’s the kind of villain the TCU deserves, which is not to ignore Englund, whose devilish take on Jiminy is like a bug-sized Freddy Krueger if he were more interested in mentoring than maiming. Together, these veteran genre baddies ensure Pinocchio Unstrung stays balanced between shock-and-awe laughs and the rooted evil that lurks in the shadows, pulling Pinocchio’s strings without the not-so-real boy even knowing.

There are rougher aspects that pull the film back down to Earth. Despite how far Frake-Waterfield’s TCU has advanced — and while it’s inspiring to see the franchise reinvest box office returns into better quality products — Pinocchio Unstrung can’t escape some wonky digital backgrounds. Granted, they’re leaps and bounds beyond the work in Winnie-the-Pooh: Blood and Honey, but there are cracks in execution. Some supporting dialogue is hammy, jokes don’t always land, and other horror-comedy pitfalls stack on top. Again, it’s a massive upgrade for the TCU… but there’s more room to develop.

items.title: Samsung Galaxy Z Fold 8 Ultra Review So Far
items.author: Jacqueline Thomas
items.content:
Earlier today, Samsung launched the Galaxy Z Fold 8 Ultra, along with the Fold 8 and the Flip 8. Now, aside from tacking ‘Ultra’ onto the name, the Z Fold 8 Ultra looks and feels a lot like last year’s Z Fold 7. I’ve even put them next to each other, and apart from last year’s much more vivid colorway, I legitimately couldn’t tell the difference between them.

However, appearances are only skin deep, and Samsung has made some big improvements over its last-generation foldables, like finally including the same 5000mAh battery you’d find in the Galaxy S26 Ultra that came out earlier this year. That doesn’t necessarily justify adding the Ultra to the name, but I suspect it has something to do with the Fold 8 Ultra’s new $2099 price tag.

Samsung’s flagship foldables have never exactly been affordable, but crossing that $2000 threshold, just to start, is a lot to ask for a smartphone. Samsung sent me the Fold 8 Ultra for review, and while I don’t know if it’s worth more than two grand, it does incredible. I’ll be spending a lot more time with the device over the next week or so to see exactly how it lines up, but in the meantime I’m going to share my impressions here.

Last year, the Samsung Galaxy Z Fold 7 was drastically slimmed down compared to the Z Fold 6. Samsung says it’s repeated that success, claiming that the Z Fold 8 Ultra is its thinnest foldable yet, but even when I have the Fold 7 and the Fold 8 Ultra right next to each other, I really can’t tell the difference.

In truth, Samsung shaved just 0.1mm off of the Z Fold 8 Ultra, making it just 4.1mm thick when unfolded and 8.9mm thick when folded. And, all credit to Samsung, that does make it the thinnest foldable the company has made, but by such a small margin that you’d never actually notice. With a phone this thin, especially a folding one, durability is a huge concern, but it seems like Samsung thought about that.

Now, I never had any issue with the Fold 7’s build, and I doubt the Fold 8 would fare any worse with 0.1 mm shaved off the chassis, but Samsung added a new titanium alloy film beneath the display, which should help prevent the phone from folding in ways that aren’t indicated by Samsung.

The Fold 8 Ultra’s display seems largely the same as the Fold 7’s as well, with a 6.5-inch cover display at 1080 x 2520, and an unfolded 8-inch display with a resolution of 2504 x 2256. The main improvement here is that the unfolded display has a slightly higher resolution than the Fold 7. Again, not really a huge deal.

However, while the crease in the unfolded display is still noticeable when you hold it at a certain angle, it’s the least pronounced it’s ever been. Seriously, looking at the display head-on, it almost seems like I'm looking at an actual tablet, rather than a foldable phone. But, then the display turns off and I look at it slightly from the side, and the crease shows itself again. Either way, though, the crease smaller than the Fold 7, so I consider that a win.

Around the back, though, is where the Galaxy Z Fold 8 Ultra actually differentiates itself from its predecessor. Last year’s model only had a 12MP ultrawide lens paired with its 200MP main lens, whereas the Fold 8 Ultra bumps the ultrawide lens up to 50MP, matching the S26 Ultra’s spec. I haven’t had a chance to play with the camera much, but the Fold 8 Ultra be able to take better pictures when you’re far away from your subject.

The Samsung Galaxy Z Fold 8 Ultra is powered by the Qualcomm Snapdragon 8 Gen 5, just like the Galaxy S26 Ultra that debuted at the beginning of 2026. I’ve only had a short time testing the Fold 8 Ultra so far, but in those few hours of poking around, the phone has been incredibly snappy. But there’s a catch.

Due to how thin the Fold 8 Ultra is, the high-end processor heats the chassis up incredibly quickly. Even before I started pushing it in a game or another heavy workload, when all I was doing on it was installing a bunch of apps, I noticed that the phone was very warm to the touch. Now, to be fair, when I’m reviewing a phone I tend to just rapid-fire install everything at once, so the Fold 8 Ultra was doing some work.

When I booted up Arknights Endfield, I noticed that the phone got even warmer, to the point where it was uncomfortable to hold. You can mitigate this by putting a case on the phone, but even the display can start to feel warm when you’ve been playing games for a while. This was a problem with the Fold 7 as well, and while I got used to it last year, it’s a little disappointing that Samsung hasn’t found a way to dissipate heat a little better. But I guess everything’s in service to having a super thin phone.

What I’m worried about as I get ready to benchmark the Z Fold 8 Ultra is how much the Snapdragon 8 Elite Gen 5 will throttle. Pretty much every flagship smartphone over the last couple years throttles to some degree, but with how little space the Fold 8 Ultra has to dissipate heat, it’s likely that its SoC will lose a good bit of performance during long workloads.

Samsung claims that it’s improved the cooling solution with a new graphite cooling structure, which supposedly helps dissipate heat. But, there’s only so much Samsung can do with a phone this thin. Luckily, until you boot up a demanding game, most things you do with a smartphone are bursty enough that you don’t have to worry about throttling. As soon as you start playing around with CapCut or playing games, though, be prepared for a hot slab of glass in your hand.

The Galaxy Z Fold 8 Ultra does finally include a better battery, too. For years, Samsung has been shoving slightly smaller batteries into its foldables to save space, but the company has found a way to include a 5000mAh battery in the Fold 8 Ultra, rather than the 4400mAh battery found in the Fold 7. Samsung claims that the phone will last for 27 hours of video playback, and I’ll believe that when I see it, but either way it should last a bit longer than the Fold 7. This, along with the much-improved camera are the only things that really feel ‘Ultra’ about the Fold 8 Ultra. That’ll be enough for some folks, but either way, a $2,100 price tag will be hard to justify.

Instead, it feels like the Fold 8 Ultra is the true followup to the Fold 7, with the ‘Ultra’ branding tacked on to make the $100 higher price seem reasonable. I won’t know whether or not the Fold 8 Ultra is worth its high asking price until I do some more testing, but for most people, the regular Fold 8 probably makes more sense. After all, it has the exact same chipset, just with a thicker chassis, a smaller screen and a slightly worse camera.

Jackie Thomas is the Hardware and Buying Guides Editor at IGN and the PC components queen. You can follow her

items.title: Avatar Legends: The Fighting Game Review
items.author: Mitchell Saltzman
items.content:
Okay, we’ve got a bit of a good news/bad news situation here. Good news first: Without hyperbole, Avatar Legends is one of the most fun fighting games I’ve ever played. There’s something special about the way these characters move, the variety in their playstyles, their incredible animations, the flexibility of their combat options, and the satisfying ways they can execute some of the sickest combos I’ve ever seen. In so many ways, this is game Avatar: The Last Airbender has deserved, as well as the old-school 1-on-1 fighter that hardcore fighting game fans have been clamoring for recently. But now the bad news: That stellar gameplay does some pretty heavy lifting, as the package around it is lacking in surprising ways. From an inability to change the difficulty level of the AI in single player, to a cumbersome training mode that’s missing standard features, to lobbies that can only support two players, the list goes on and on. Like Aang and Korra at the beginning of their stories, Avatar Legends is rough and unpolished, but beaming with potential.

The best way to describe Avatar Legends in fighting game terms is that it’s a fast-paced, mobility-driven 1v1 fighter, focusing on creative combo expression, a high bar of execution, and the uniqueness of each character as opposed to powerful, universal system mechanics. All of that to say, this is one of the most difficult 1v1 fighting games to learn, but the payoff once you’re able to actually perform even a fraction of what these characters are capable of is extraordinary.

The fundamentals of the fighting system are simple at a glance: there are three attack buttons, covering light, medium, and heavy strikes, and then a “Flow” button that is generally tied to each character’s unique movement options. As is the case with many anime fighting games, there’s a basic logic of being able to combo a light into a medium into a heavy into a special move, and there’s even an auto-combo for those that just want to mash one button and get some respectable damage.

But it’s that Flow button that really opens up Avatar’s fighting system and makes it truly special. Every character has a very particular way of moving around the screen, and these mobility options can be absolutely wild. Aang, for instance, can press forward-Flow for a quick forward hop that has three different follow-ups, all of which have wildly different uses. Or he can press Flow and a direction in the air to take out his glider to attack from unpredictable and awkward angles, control the direction of the wind to affect his momentum in the air, or fast fall from the sky, just to name a few of his techniques.

Each fighter has their own way of breaking the rules of this system.

Meanwhile, Azula can air dash in eight directions twice before touching the ground (in just one of her forms); Toph can shift the earth to pull opponents towards her, or herself towards opponents; Katara can charge special moves while being able to slide across the ground – and that just scratches the surface of what these characters can do. Each of the 12 fighters on the roster has their own way of breaking the rules of this system, and it makes each one feel incredibly powerful and satisfying to learn.

In addition to the Flow meter that governs all of those crazy movement techniques, there’s also a well designed energy meter governing EX special move and super usage. Instead of being a bar that fills up as you take or receive damage, the energy meter is segmented into individual points, and you only get a maximum of one point per combo. There are other ways to gain energy as well, including character specific “critical hits” that reward you for performing certain skillful feats. I love having to make on the fly decisions about how to build and spend resources, and between both meters, Avatar Legends’ fighting system is one of the very best when it comes to that aspect of fighting games.

As a big Avatar fan, another aspect of the design that I love is how closely each kit embodies the spirit of the character. In the show, Korra is highly skilled and powerful from the very start, but she’s also reckless and tends to wind up in bad situations after acting without thinking. Here, that’s reflected with a very hard-hitting playstyle that can deal a lot of damage, but also comes with a lot of risk. Her high damage combo routes can cost of lot of meter, she has risky tools that most other characters don’t (like a meterless invincible reversal), and her special moves can all be enhanced for more damage at the cost of even more meter, which makes it very easy to accidentally wind up in the highly detrimental “unbalanced” state that’s caused by emptying your entire Flow meter.

I love how closely each kit embodies the spirit of each character.

Azula, on the other hand, has two completely different forms: Focused and Firelord. Focused embodies Azula’s calm and collected demeanor that we see throughout most of the show, and that’s reflected with a fighting style that requires a lot of precision, with much of her damage locked behind special move extensions that require button presses within a one or two frame window. That’s contrasted by her Firelord form, which represents her total meltdown toward the end of the series. Her precisely timed special move extensions go out the window, her air dashes become slower but cover more distance, she does more damage with fewer hits, and ultimately just becomes an absolute close-range monster that can smother you with overwhelming force.

That’s not all, as the entire roster also has three support characters that can be chosen before a match begins to change up elements of their kits, much like the character variations from Mortal Kombat X. These can empower specific special moves in ways that open up new combo routes, grant you new special moves entirely, or improve your movement techniques. All of these systems work together to make what is a fairly small roster on paper feel much bigger.

Then The Fire Nation Attacked

Avatar Legends’ story mode is an original tale outlined by series creator Michael Dante DiMartino and written by Tim Hedrick, who has written many episodes of both The Last Airbender and The Legend of Korra, along with several other pieces of canonical Avatar content. Despite that authenticity, however, it mostly feels like fanfiction fluff. It begins with Aang waking up in an alternate universe where he doesn’t exist, people who shouldn’t exist in the same time period are best buds, and everyone is basically the polar opposite of how they normally act. There are a handful of fun callbacks, and the voice acting is quite good despite not featuring almost any of the original cast, but the overall story never sank its hooks into me, which made the whopping 27 chapter campaign overstay its welcome by the time I hit chapter 15 or so.

Outside of the lackluster story mode, there’s really not much else here to hold the attention of anyone who isn’t interested in competing against other people online. There’s a traditional arcade mode, but no ability to adjust the difficulty of the AI, so if the matches are either too easy or too hard, you’re out of luck.

On the teaching tools front, Avatar Legends is kind of all over the place. It’s great that there’s a frame meter turned on by default to immediately help you understand the frame data of a move, as well as an option to turn hit boxes on so you can understand the actual range and vulnerability of an attack. But on the flipside, doing simple things in training mode like setting a dummy to jab after blocking is extremely cumbersome thanks to a poor UI; and, shockingly, there is no way to set the dummy to block randomly, making it very difficult to practice hit confirms.

The combo trials at least are pretty good at teaching practical combos at the novice and intermediate ranks, and a clever “Breakdown” feature allows you to go through a combo step by step – the screen freezes for each input, allowing you to get the muscle memory of a combo down without having to worry about the timing. What’s really cool though is that in addition to combo trials, there are character specific tutorials that actually teach you how to best make use of a character’s tools to pressure opponents, open them up with mix-ups, or defend against their offense. I do think these tutorials could do a bit better of a job easing you into the nutty stuff that characters can do, but nonetheless, it’s a great idea and I wish more fighting games would spend as much time teaching you how to land the hits that enable combos as they do on the actual combos themselves.

Finally, my experience thus far with online play has been excellent, with smooth rollback netcode and a very snappy pace thanks to quick rematches, lightning fast load times, and instant returns to practice mode if the match drops due to a disconnect. That said, online also has some very surprising omissions that should be standard in a modern fighting game. The worst offender is the lack of lobby support for more than two players, which means you can’t set up a room to play with more than one friend where two people play and the rest watch and await their turn. This is apparently planned for a post-launch update, but it’s a glaring omission right out of the gate. Less shocking, but still worth pointing out, is the lack of replay search, which is used to find replays of high-level players so you can see how they handle specific matchups, nor is there replay takeover. Both features would be nice to have, especially in a game with so much variation in how its characters can be played.

items.title: The Walking Dead: Dead City Season 3 Review
items.author: Matt Fowler
items.content:
This is a non-spoiler review for all eight episodes of The Walking Dead: Dead City Season 3. The third season premieres Sunday, July 26 at 9pm ET/PT on AMC and AMC+.

The Walking Dead: Dead City has finally stumbled upon a mostly-winning formula for its third season, delivering compelling new elements and a bold new direction that should have been part of the show's DNA from the start. Outside of a messy premiere episode "reboot," of sorts, the Season 3 story — and where we find Maggie and Negan as frenemies-to-friends — is what this series should have been all along.

Season 3 is as close to a do-over as the show can muster given the sloppy tapestry of the last few years with the Croat, the Dama, bitter Hershel, and New Babylon — and with Maggie and Negan pretending like they already hadn't come to a friendly coexistence by the end of the mothership Walking Dead's final season. Hopefully, you'll forgive the initial hastiness to undo the missteps of the first two seasons, but once that dust settles (literally, kind of), the new path our characters find themselves on is quite good. And in the back half of Season 3, you're going to see a phenomenal episode unlike anything the Walking Dead universe has ever done before.

Season 3 is far less cluttered than the first two, and keep in mind that the end of Season 2 was working to reset the missteps of Season 1, awkwardly shuttering that weird Last of Us-style apocalypse father/daughter set up with Negan and Ginny — a bonehead idea meant to ride the coattails of a trend at the expense of Negan's own previous personal growth and new family. Pairing Maggie and Negan up for a new series was a grand idea, but Dead City started off the blocks by pretending that Walking Dead: Season 11 never happened, and now we've finally gotten to the point where it's narratively acknowledging that was a dickhead move.

In a zompocalypse story, there's only one true endgame. Posed as a question, it's: "How do we rebuild?" Dead City now goes a step beyond that and explores whether or not a true utopia is really possible. Should a community always have to live in fear, in hiding? Is a non-lethal approach to community relations actually achievable? Scarcity causes panic and conflict, so can we actually get to a place of comfort and camaraderie — something better than even Alexandria, Hilltop, et al.?

Even if the thought of "Neggie" makes you wretch, you have to admit these two characters are connected at the deepest level.

Manhattan continues serving as the backdrop here as Maggie and newcomer siblings nicely played by Aimee Garcia and Raúl Castillo try to create a community that not only thrives, but also clearly advertises itself to the world as a beacon of hope. This attempt to earnestly move forward means that past scars must get buried, and it allows Lauren Cohan to tackle her best Maggie material in years, confronting her grief over Glenn and finally settling into a dynamic with Negan that makes sense given all they've endured and experienced together.

Admittedly, I'm a "Neggie" shipper; I'm a sucker for this particular slow-burn enemies-to-? team-up. I would be over the moon if the "?" meant lovers or even smoochers, but I get why the show wouldn't want to go there. I also completely understand why most sane people, tried and true romance tropes aside, still can't forgive Negan and would never ever want this. That being said, and without spoiling some great things, Season 3 breaks down several walls and offers up intriguing, emotional avenues and scenarios you won't expect. I was simultaneously delighted and deflated, so make as much sense of that as you can. At the very least, even if the thought of "Neggie" makes you wretch, you have to admit these two characters are connected at the deepest level. That's the itch Season 3 is finally scratching, and not to sensationalize or make headlines either. It's thoughtful and wonderful and the one aforementioned back half episode is the best thing the Dead universe has done in years.

Jeffrey Dean Morgan's Negan, who has some pretty bad "shittin' pants" Negan-isms this season (oof), is a square peg in a sea of round holes, believing in Maggie's new dream and wanting the best for her, but also too trapped in his own pessimism to be anything other than adjacent to it all. Though now that Dead City has matured past these two being at each other's throats, and even past their uneasy frenemy phase, Negan figures himself as Maggie's protector even if she never asked for it and even if it undermines her new societal values. Negan's not off to the side sulking, no; he does however find himself slinking back into wastoid slacker Negan mode, befriending Dillard (Jimmi Simpson) — a former cop who's been living in a bar in some pretty harsh isolation and who's had to twist his psyche into some unique shapes in order to survive emotionally. It's a fun but complicated bromance.

Season 3 doesn't have a long-form villain; there's a third act antagonist, but for the most part, the struggle involves trying to be a bright virtuous light in a swamp of sin and selfishness. The season isn't a home run, considering the embarrassing heavy lifting done in the premiere to wipe the slate, and the messaging at the very end feels a little reductive and regressive. Nevertheless, the bulk of it is rich and meaningful; the new characters are well presented and well tended, some clunky old characters become non-issues, and Maggie and Negan — at long last — have the relationship they should have had since Dead City began.

items.title: X-Men '97 Season 2, Episode 6 Review & Recap: "Danger.exe"
items.author: Jesse Schedeen
items.content:
Warning: This review contains full spoilers for X-Men '97 Season 2, Episode 6!

At what point does Disney need to change the name of this series? It may be X-Men '97, but the show is really starting to lean into the early 2000s era of the comics with Episode 6. That being said, when the result is that X-Men '97 starts drawing from all-time classics like the Grant Morrison/Frank Quitely New X-Men and Joss Whedon/John Cassaday Astonishing X-Men runs, who are we to complain?

As is the show's wont, "Danger.exe" covers a lot of ground in the span of 30 minutes. This episode pulls in many elements from the first half of the aforementioned Astonishing X-Men, albeit with Polaris (Carolina Ravassa) filling in for Kitty Pryde. Meanwhile, on the New X-Men front, we get the introduction of those lovely Quitely-designed costumes and some key developments late in the episode. It's a stew with a lot of ingredients, but they all come together nicely enough in the end. This isn't a case like Episode 5, where the show failed to truly take advantage of the source material it was adapting.

One of the main draws of "Danger.exe" is that we finally get to see the Xavier Institute treated as what it actually is - a school for young mutants. That's something X-Men '97 and its predecessor always tended to gloss over in the past, probably because that element wasn't a big priority for the comics back in 1992, either. It's great to see the show making up for lost time, as we get a new influx of youngsters pulled from various eras of the X-lore. Naturally, that includes Quentin Quire (Thomas Dekker) sporting his trademark "Magneto Was Right" shirt. Fingers crossed for a "Riot at Xavier's" adaptation at some point down the road.

Continuing from the foundation laid in Episode 2, this chapter delivers a strong, focused character arc for Polaris, as she makes her full break from X-Factor and tries to find her place in a world that fears her becoming the spitting image of her late father. She slides neatly into Kitty's prodigal daughter role, and the episode fleshes out her relationship with Professor Xavier (Ross Marquand) in satisfying fashion. There's a complaint to be made that the series has enough characters in its ensemble cast already without inducting another X-Man, but when the results are this enjoyable, does it really matter?

Speaking of Xavier, this episode also thrives in exploring the fallout of his tragic ordeal in Egypt and the death of his beloved Magnus. That all dovetails nicely into the introduction of Danger (Rachel Kimsey), as one of Xavier's most powerful tools becomes sentient and goes rogue. It's interesting that this episode shies away from the darker aspects of the Xavier/Danger backstory. In the comics, it's revealed that the good Professor knew she had become sentient and kept her enslaved anyway, part of a concerted effort by early-'00s X-Men writers to paint the X-Men's founder in a less flattering light. Here, I don't know that such a reveal was needed, so it's probably just as well that the X-Men '97 writer's room didn't go down that road. Xavier has enough blood on his hands as it is. Plus, this episode isn't shy about foreshadowing Onslaught as a future development.

"Danger.exe" culminates in a terrific battle sequence in the ruins of Genosha, one that again showcases just how strong the animation is in this series. And though Danger herself seems to be a one-and-done villain, there is some significant fallout. Xavier finds a way to honor his old friend by reclaiming the ruins of Genosha for mutantkind and spearheading X-Corp (there's that New X-Men influence again). I'm enjoying how much the series is taking advantage of the fraught political climate post-Operation: Zero Tolerance. If the X-Men can't exist on American shores any longer, they'll just plant their flag somewhere else.

So it seems that the new status quo going forward is one where we have one group of X-Men operating as teachers at the Institute and another carrying out missions from Genosha. Sort of a Blue Team/Gold Team divide, in other words. It's an intriguing new setup, though there is the eternal concern about whether the series is taking on too much at once.
