At some point, X-Men '97 really needs to grow beyond these limited 9-10 episode seasons.

Finally, we get a big tease on the Apocalypse (Marquand) front, as we see the mutant tyrant make good on his promise to resurrect Gambit (A. J. LoCascio). It's a cool scene, partiularly with how it transforms Gambit's imagery from that of playing cards to tarot cards. I can't wait to see how the series handles the big Death reveal.

items.title: Splatoon Raiders Review
items.author: Logan Plant
items.content:
My wrists are sore from how much Splatoon Raiders I’ve been playing. If you’re a series veteran like I am, you know that can happen when you combine its signature twitchy motion aiming with marathon PvP sessions. But there’s no competitive multiplayer here. Instead, the first Splatoon spinoff is an incredibly well-designed action RPG that’s already drawn me in for more than 40 hours. It’s all about tinkering: I love crafting my build to get just a little bit stronger, putting it to the test in challenging, varied stages, and then sifting through the meaningful loot I earned to see what creative combination I can glue together next. When you stack that classic but expertly executed loop on top of Splatoon’s already awesome foundation, you get the strongest campaign this series has ever seen and one of the best Switch 2 games so far.

Raiders follows Splatoon 3’s idols, Shiver, Frye, and Big Man, as they head to the remote Spirhalite Islands to hunt for treasure. They’re trying to help pay off Splatsville’s enormous debt by battling waves of Salmonid enemies to uncover valuable relics. The story that unfolds is a swashbuckling ride that adds plenty of interesting details to Splatoon’s excellent world. Beyond the plot, though, I’m most impressed with how every element of this setting plays into the central theme of making the most of what’s already in front of you.

That resourcefulness starts with your character: the Mechanic, a blank slate Inkling or Octoling who has a knack for crafting. All 11 ink-dishing weapon types return, but the Mechanic has put their own spin on the designs. Instead of the polished, toy-like look of the mainline Splatoon games, weapons in Raiders are stitched together with duct tape and literal garbage, which gives it a scrappy, homemade visual identity that really stands out. The standard Splattershot’s ink tank can take the form of old bottles of soda or dish soap, while the bow-and-arrow-like Stringer can be made out of a broken tennis racket.

Attacking enemies, covering the ground with ink, and swimming through it feels better than ever no matter which weapon type you prefer, thanks in part to the improved HD Rumble of the Switch 2 pro controller. It was fun to experiment with different weapons across the 18-hour campaign and well beyond, even if I found myself falling back into using the dual-wielded pistol Dualies, which were hard to break away from after more than 150 hours spent with them in Splatoon 3.

Even though I mostly stuck to the weapon I’m used to, Raiders still felt super fresh thanks to its standout new feature: Gadgets. These are special pieces of equipment separate from your main weapon, and a huge chunk of the RPG loop is built around them. The options are spread across three classes determined by which ink tank you equip – Speed, Power, and Tactical – and each one’s unique strengths are endlessly fun to experiment with. The Speed Tank’s Gadgets prioritize mobility and evasiveness, such as the Jump Bomb that lets you leap out of dangerous situations, or the Flywire grappling hook that can close the distance between you and your target in a flash. The Power Tank’s Gadgets let you charge in to deal heavy damage, like the Splatchet’s sweeping slash that can kill some Salmonids in a single blow. But my favorite has to be the Tactical Tank, with strategic Gadgets that focus on spacing. That includes the Bombloons: ink-filled explosives that link up with each other, causing a violent burst that scales with how many you have in the field.

Gadgets are deeply customizable tools that allow for tons of different builds.

Each individual power is just the beginning, as these are deeply customizable tools that allow you to create tons of different builds, especially when you consider that you can have two Gadgets equipped at one time. You’ll constantly uncover new combinations that play off each other in smart ways – like how the Jump Bomb and Flywire can work together to make an acrobatic build where you never touch the ground. As you complete missions, you’ll unlock new skills for your Gadgets as well, and this is where the experimentation truly shines.

Take the Bombloons, for example. You could work on making them as explosive as possible, covering more area when they detonate and dealing more damage. Or, you could take advantage of the fact that the tether between the Bombloons also deals damage, and instead work to make them last as long as possible when deployed to maximize that tether. These are just two of hundreds of potential avenues you could take, and as your character levels up, you unlock room to slot even more skills into each Gadget, further expanding your potential. I will say, the crafting menu could really use a wishlist system so I can keep better track of all the materials I need to buy these upgrades – right now, I have to screenshot, write down, or just remember everything I’m hunting for.

You can swap your Tank type at any time, and you are actively encouraged to do so, with specific missions that yield unique rewards depending on which one you use. That means you always want to rotate between them and keep each Gadget lineup up to speed with your current level. I have at least one strategy centered around each tank, like my favorite Tactical build where I literally never fire my main weapon because I stay submerged in the ink 100% of the time, cooling down my gadgets faster and causing thousands of points of passive damage. The way Gadgets interact with each other inspires creativity, and new build ideas constantly spark in my head as I try to figure out more ways to break Raiders open. And it never stops giving you new toys to play with or ways to upgrade your gear. Every time I thought, “wouldn’t it be cool if I could do ?”, I eventually unlocked that possibility.

This campaign forces you to use every tool to the fullest, because the Salmonid army is not messing around. It’s one of the more challenging first-party Nintendo games in the last several years. Relentless hordes will overwhelm you if you don’t head in with a solid strategy, and your base weapon just won’t cut it on its own. If that sounds unappealing to you, there are three difficulty levels to choose from, and all they change is how much damage you take – not the level design, rewards, or experience given. As an S+ rank player in Splatoon 3, I immediately turned the difficulty to the hardest setting, Survivalist. This led to tense firefights and a thin margin between victory and defeat, and I love the stakes that created. I also tested the standard mode, and while I didn’t die nearly as much, the smart encounter design and identical number of enemies still provided a satisfying learning curve. Failure is an important part of Raiders. When you lose, you have to head back to base, rethink your build, and maybe complete a couple of side missions before trying again. The deep customization wouldn’t be nearly as interesting if the missions were a breeze, and Raiders completely nails that important balance.

It also doesn’t hold your hand at all. Each stage has a threat level it warns you of ahead of time, but it doesn’t stop you from tackling something way above your paygrade. You may get crushed, but it’s so fulfilling when you find a way to topple a mission you had no business clearing yet, made even better when you’re rewarded with a rare weapon or material drop for doing so. Missions only take a few minutes each, so it wasn’t frustrating or a huge setback when I lost. There were several times I could tell I was inching closer and closer to the finish line until I finally broke through. Not just from the numbers going up, which you do visually see, but from a tangible feeling that I was taking enemies down a split second faster, my health was lasting a little bit longer, and I had better Gadgets ready to go.

Relentless hordes of Salmonids will overwhelm you if you don’t head in with a solid strategy.

Raiders stands out even further with its nearly perfect pacing. Across its campaign, I grew incrementally stronger with each success, but the Salmonid army always stayed one step ahead, with increasingly challenging stages that asked me to find new ways to keep up. But I never felt like I was unnecessarily grinding or replaying levels over and over, because there were always new stages to play.

There are five main level types, each with a different objective and unique rewards for clearing them. The standard levels are linear paths with some fun platforming challenges as you make your way to the next intense wave of Salmonids, grabbing optional collectibles as you go. You’ll also dive into multi-level dungeons where you need to clear each floor in a limited amount of time, placing an emphasis on efficiency and raw damage output over pure survival. Alternatively, Salmonid Dens star a specific enemy type and often require a degree of lateral thinking where picking the right tool in your toolbox is the only way to clear the room in time, like a race to the top of a mountain where the Speed Tank is the best choice to get the job done. And then there are levels about mining a giant crystal for resources, where you only need to fill the meter halfway to clear the stage, but can risk it all to fill it further for bonus rewards, as long as you’re confident you can still extract before losing all of your lives.

Finally, the Sampler Dungeons are a standout, as they are Splatoon’s take on shrines from Breath of the Wild. Each one takes you through a set of puzzles where you’re limited to a specific loadout. What’s smart is that there’s a Sampler Dungeon for each Gadget, and it equips you with one of the strongest possible versions of that tool. Then, through the puzzles, Raiders essentially teaches you why this Gadget is good, and gives you different ideas for how you could use it. They are great tutorials for new powers, and I learned things about multiple gadgets I’d previously underestimated. These are motivating, too: seeing some of the craziest combos you can pull off with a souped up version of a Gadget made me want to go and upgrade my own so I could do it all the time.

I really enjoy every level type for different reasons, and enemy variety shines across the board. The Salmonids will be familiar to anyone who played a lot of Salmon Run in Splatoon 2 or 3, but a key difference is that Raiders focuses on handcrafted levels rather than random waves, and the way they’re used leads to clever encounters that often feel like puzzles. Every enemy type poses a unique problem, like the Smoker‘s giant bubble that protects enemy Salmonids, or the bouncing Big Shot‘s damaging shock wave that pressures you out of the ink. Different combinations of these foes means there’s always a lot to consider as you weave through the chaos and make split-second decisions about who to take out first – maybe you surround yourself with protection by way of the orbiting Splatellite Gadget, or you could use updrafts nearby to get airborne. There are always multiple ways to win.

I really enjoy every level type, each for different reasons.

And when you do win, each of these mission types gives you something different as a reward, whether it’s money, a new buff, certain crafting materials to improve your Gadgets, or skill points to upgrade your character. So when you need something specific, there’s always a level type waiting for you, and you’re constantly bouncing between them. When you add in the endless build shuffling and a few excellent boss battles sprinkled throughout, Raiders always feels fresh.

There are also Spicy versions of every level that dramatically up the difficulty and number of enemies. These are extremely fun to tackle and require even more creativity and strategy to clear. I was initially unimpressed by Raiders’ graphics – it doesn’t look all that different from a Switch 1 game – but when I saw the sheer amount of enemies crammed on screen in a Spicy level while it stayed locked at 60fps, I understood how this game was taking advantage of the Switch 2’s additional power. My main complaint is that these levels aren’t that visually interesting. You’ll see a lot of repeat biomes with a fairly bland, muted color palette, which is an odd thing to say about this generally very stylish series. Compared to the bright, neon stages of the main series’ multiplayer levels, the underground subway look of the Octo Expansion, and the Side Order DLC’s monochrome style, this is definitely my least favorite Splatoon aesthetic.

Raiders is playable entirely in online co-op, and I’m so impressed with how it gets pretty much every decision right. This series has come a long way considering Splatoon 1 and 2 didn't even have a competent friends lobby system. In fact, Splatoon has shed a lot of its bad habits here, as there is no lengthy newscast rigamarole to mash through when you boot it up – you just jump right in.

Multiplayer supports up to four raiders, and while the host has to pick the levels, everyone earns their own separate loot and gets credit for completing that stage, so you can feasibly play the whole campaign together. The smartest thing it does is level scaling based on the individual player. If I’m level 30 playing in my friend’s level eight mission, enemies appear as level 30 on my screen and level eight on theirs. I also earn rewards appropriate for my level, which makes it worth it for me to play through lower-tiered stages I’ve already cleared before. That meant I both had to put up a real fight and I wouldn’t ruin my friends' game by just streamrolling everything with endgame gear. And because difficulty settings only impact the amount of damage you take, I can play on Survivalist while my friend plays on an easier difficulty with no issue.

If you’re a solo player and need help, you can call for assistance from other players around the world, who will earn bonus rewards for helping someone in need. There’s even an arcade game to play back at your base while you wait for everyone to finish their prep, which is a nice throwback to Splatoon 1’s Squid Jump on the Wii U GamePad. It really feels like they thought of everything.

That said, I do think Raiders is actually slightly better as a single-player game, only because its clever level designs can be trivialized when you throw more firepower into the mix, which turns it into a bit more of a generic multiplayer shooter. Don’t get me wrong, it’s still totally fun to blast through levels with your friends if that’s what you want to do, it’s just a very different vibe to the puzzle-like nature of going it alone. I might recommend you play the campaign on your own first, and then group up with others when you go back for the endgame.

Speaking of which, the endgame opens up even more difficult challenges to tackle and more powerful customization to gear you up for it. I’ve already put in 20 hours since the credits rolled. It’s totally reasonable to feel satisfied and walk away after the awesome campaign, but if you’re like me and play Monster Hunter or similar games for hundreds of hours and love the grind, there’s plenty of really cool stuff left to do. I initially thought the balance wasn’t quite as finely-tuned in the endgame – it seemed much easier to craft a pretty broken build fairly fast – but Raiders eventually rebutted with tests that sent me right back to the workbench. Raiders is unlikely to be a forever game for me, but I’m sure I’ll get a couple dozen more hours out of it as I play with friends, help strangers, and continue to improve my loadouts.

items.title: Her Private Hell Review
items.author: Arnold T. Blumberg
items.content:
It's been a decade since Nicolas Winding Refn last released a feature film. Bronson, Valhalla Rising, Drive, Only God Forgives, and The Neon Demon — what a run. Most of the last ten years were spent tethered to streaming giants, developing Too Old to Die Young for Amazon and Copenhagen Cowboy for Netflix. The Danish filmmaker admits that, as his nearly decade-long episodic adventure was winding down, he felt there was "nothing else left" in his career.

Her Private Hell marks the return of a creator imbued with new life in the truest definition of a second chance. It's Refnized to the core — if anything, his striking visuals and ethereal signatures have only intensified. Post-death clarity translates into another neon-blazing pastiche of sensuality, electric colorization, and elegant madness that is quite certainly not for everyone. It's a story about daddy issues, delusional actresses, and leathered-up villains as only he can tell… for better and worse. As is common for Refn, Her Private Hell isn't one-size-fits-all; one size fits Refn, and he doesn't care if you're along for the ride or gone in sixty seconds.

Sophie Thatcher stars as starlet Elle, a hot young thing filming a throwback "space babes" picture in a futuristic, Blade Runner 2049-y metropolis. Her ex-best friend and co-star, Dominique (Havana Rose Liu), is married to her playboy-projecting father, Johnny Thunders (Dougray Scott). Then there's Private K (Charles Melton), an American GI concurrently searching for a serial murderer — a lady-killing figure known as the Leatherman. As Elle's extravagant lifestyle unravels, she finds herself in Private K's orbit, and thus the Leatherman's as well. Elle will either make it out alive or become another torn-apart statistic.

Refn's sense of framing, color palette, and cinematic style are undeniable. Her Private Hell is a Neo-Japanese giallo daydream boasting a mix of cotton-candy hues, 1960s science-fiction retrofuturism, and runway-model fashions. The purple-pink skylines, the Leatherman's tight-fitting gimp chic, the high-society grandeur that reveals a predatory underbelly — it's all so sharply imagined. Cinematographer Magnus Nordenhof Jønck reteams with Refn after Copenhagen Cowboy, and it's like they're operating the camera as one symbiotic entity. The dazzling shot compositions that Refn used to win over Hollywood are impossible to ignore; his illustrations, to zero shock, are not compromised.

Refn's penchant for drifting through nebulous storytelling concepts is a boon to Her Private Hell. It's a film with ideas: a Shakespearean fairy tale about immortal planes; a Bunraku-esque slasher with gangster flashes; a seedy Hollywood commentary about bastards preying on women. The film's perspective swaps between subplots vying for top billing like a wobbly circus performer juggling slippery balls. They all intersect — there's some method to Refn's madness, after all — yet Her Private Hell feels strangely disjointed as pieces covered in sweat, glitter, and blood meet with friction.

Sophie Thatcher is infallible as Elle, whose performance spirals into a complex examination of her insecurities.

The biggest rub is the initial gap between Elle's problems with Dominique and "Daddy," and Private K's action-forward investigation. Elle's focus is on glamour and manipulation, corrupted by the competition between Johnny Thunders' ladies, while Private K is kicking down backroom doors and hunting a hunky psychopath. You can see where both characters will inevitably collide, but the nature of their dueling narratives is hardly in harmony. You can sense Refn basking in his film's buzzy glow, loving every second and soaking it all in, but that's also why the story feels like it keeps us at arm's length, unfolding at its own wobbly pace.

If Her Private Hell is a practice of self-indulgence, Thatcher is infallible as Elle, whose performance spirals into a complex examination of her insecurities. The balance between appearing nonchalant and the dangerous pitfalls of sleazy industry dynamics becomes an unwinnable game as Elle clashes with Dominique or rolls her eyes at Kristine Froseth's naive newbie to the group, Hunter. But then in steps Melton, in uniform and with a death glare, to wash away all the bitterness and boozing with his mercenary's mission to inflict brutal justice. He's a stone-cold anti-hero with glistening abs, and equally as impressive in terms of performance power, which also goes for much of the cast. Thatcher and Melton stand tallest, yet the ensemble is well suited to Refn's approach overall.

As Her Private Hell unloads on audiences, it's Refn at his most intoxicating and elusive. He's working out demons from his past, like a gorgeous movie-within-a-movie sequence that sticks Elle on a Barbarella-type set (an adaptation Refn was courted for but never saw to completion). There's more picturesque merit in thirty seconds of Her Private Hell than other filmmakers’ entire catalog; either you're hypnotized, or trapped in your own private hell of storytelling knuckleballs. There's no in-between, because that's never been Refn. Nor should he compromise his ambitions — after all, they've gotten him this far.

items.title: MSI MPG 341CQR QD-OLED X36 Review
items.author: Bo Moore
items.content:
MSI is continuing its push into high-quality QD-OLED gaming monitors with the MSI MPG 341CQR QD-OLED X36. At $899, it serves as a true successor to one the I tested in late 2024. It comes with some upgrades to the design, extra features, and an updated panel that just gives you more of a great thing while still fitting right into that 34-inch, 3440x1440 ultra-wide curved screen format. With the latest tech and a competitive price, it’s worth a look next to earlier favorites like the or , and it can go head to head with the new .

MSI MPG 341CQR QD-OLED X36 – Design and Features

Like the MSI MPG 341CQPX QD-OLED before it, the MPG 341CQR QD-OLED X36 features a large, 34-inch ultra-wide display with a 3,440x1,440 resolution and a gentle 1800R curve that boosts immersion a touch without looking too funky for regular use. MSI has refined the design a bit, shrinking down the chin below the display and adding a special sensor, but otherwise it’s not so different. The monitor still has a basic RGB-lit segment on the back that adds little to the tame overall design, an easy-to-use five-way joystick for controls, and a power and shortcut button beside the joystick. The latter button annoyingly still defaults to launching MSI Intelligence Center (which tries to install itself automatically over USB or DisplayPort) and can’t be customized to do anything else without the app installed.

One smart change MSI made was in the stand. Rather than the wide-legged base of the earlier model, the MPG 341CQR QD-OLED X36 includes a flat, hexagonal base that doesn’t dominate so much desk space. Though it would seem less supportive at first, the stand is heavy enough in comparison to the monitor that it holds steady even while I make adjustments to the screen position. The stand supports a pretty typical range of movements with a 20-degree vertical tilt range, a 60-degree swivel range and 110mm of height adjustment. It has just a little pivot, but since the monitor is wider than the stand is tall, you can’t plan on pivoting it 90 degrees for a vertical setup. If you don’t like the stand, you can swap to 100x100mm VESA mounting. The stand has a convenient cable-routing hole in the middle to tidy things up, though it’s too small to support every cable if you filled every port on the monitor.

On the underside of the MPG 341CQR QD-OLED X36, you’ll find two HDMI 2.1 ports and a DisplayPort 2.1a port that all support the full resolution and refresh rate of the monitor. Alongside the DisplayPort and HDMI inputs, the MPG 341CQR QD-OLED X36 has some USB ports that prove highly useful. There are two USB-A ports that can link back to a desktop with a USB-B backhaul or link to a full-featured USB-C port. That USB-C port is a rockstart, supporting the full display resolution and 360Hz refresh rate while also providing up to 98 watts of power delivery. That’s incredibly handy if you ever use the monitor with a laptop. The monitor also includes a 3.5mm jack for audio output, but it doesn’t have any speakers of its own.

Just below the display is a sensor that can enable a handful of extra features. The monitor can automatically turn off the screen to conserve energy and protect the panel when you step away, and it can wake back up when you return (though it takes a few seconds to start back up). It can also automatically adjust the brightness and other settings based on ambient conditions. With all of these enabled, I found the monitor a little tedious to use, with it adjusting the picture too much and the boot-up taking a little too long for my taste, but these can be dialed in to work with the way you use a monitor. One setting could simply dim the display when I wasn’t looking at it seemingly based on eye sensing, and this seemed perfectly practical.

MSI MPG 341CQR QD-OLED X36 – Testing

The MSI MPG 341CQR QD-OLED X36 performs wonderfully in testing. By default, it runs at 70% brightness with Eco settings and an ultra-low power mode, but this still allows it to hit 205.9 nits with 100% coverage of the sRGB color space and 98% coverage of the DCI-P3 color space. The color accuracy is also impressive in this setting, with a dE Max of 2.02. Dialing up the settings to 100 percent brightness sees the display reach 308 nits while maintaining its color gamut and comfortable 6600K white point. Color accuracy dips a little, but the display still hits an average dE1976 of 1.17 and a max dE1976 of 2.21. It may not be perfect for color critical work like photo and video editing, but it won’t be far off the mark either.

As the MPG 341CQR QD-OLED X36 has an OLED panel, it naturally has infinite contrast. It also enjoys very fast pixel response times. When the display is set to 360Hz, I can’t see any ghosting in Blur Busters’ UFO test.

HDR settings give the MPG 341CQR QD-OLED X36 even more means to impress. I measured a peak white point of 366 nits for a full screen window and 603 nits for a 10% window using the True Black 500 HDR setting. With the Peak 1000 setting, the fullscreen and 10% windows didn’t change by more than a few nits, but I measured as high as 1,551 nits for a small highlight comprising less than 4% of the screen area and 900 nits for a window less than 9% of the screen area. Combine these dazzling highlights with the deep black levels, and you get remarkable visuals.

MSI MPG 341CQR QD-OLED X36 – Gaming and Performance

Gaming on the MSI MPG 341CQR QD-OLED X36 is a joy. The ultra-wide, 21:9 format gives games an extra level of immersion, by filling that much more of my field of view while the 3440x1440 resolution remains a lot easier for gaming hardware to handle than 4K. That’s important, because the MPG 341CQR QD-OLED X36 runs at up to 360Hz and supports variable refresh rates to help ensure every frame is delivered in full and screen tearing never crops up.

In practice, it delivers on that promise. 007 First Light was all the more cinematic on the wide-screen, and though it took DLSS and multi-frame generation to even come close to scratching that 360Hz ceiling, the game appeared buttery smooth, helping me dash through the higher-octane action sequences of the game with ease.

The rich contrast and bold color really help present the moody, dark scenes and explosive firefights of 007 First Light well. And the 360Hz refresh rate and ultra-fast pixel response time of the OLED panel make snapping to targets much easier as there’s basically no blurring to impede me. All that speed became even more beneficial in fast-paced games like Trepang2, helping me zip through levels and put the moves on enemies that never knew what hit them.

The display can still have some trouble with glare, which spreads out wide thanks to the curve of the monitor, but the panel has a new coating that helps keep reflected light a bit darker than MSI’s prior monitors. As long as you’re not fighting with strong direct lighting or sunlight, it’ll be pretty great looking. In my office, with overhead lighting on and some daylight trickling into the space, the screen treatment was enough to make reflections very hard to see even on a completely black screen.

Another improvement the MPG 341CQR QD-OLED X36 brings is a RGB stripe sub-pixel layout. Early OLED monitors suffer from uncommon subpixel layouts that create issues in fine details, especially text. This presents as color fringing, which can be very distracting. On the MPG 341CQR QD-OLED X36, that issue is gone. It had already been largely solved in the earlier MSI MPG 341CQPX QD-OLED, but here it’s not even a risk.

Mark Knapp is a contributing freelancer for IGN covering everything electronics and gaming hardware. He has over 10 years of experience in the tech industry with bylines at PCMag, Reviewed, CNET, and more. Find Mark on Twitter

items.title: Avatar Aang: The Last Airbender Review
items.author: Arnold T. Blumberg
items.content:
Nearly two decades after Nickelodeon’s beloved Avatar: The Last Airbender wrapped up its stellar three-season run, the original animated crew is back in action for a full-length movie, Avatar Aang: The Last Airbender. The film is big, bold, and stunningly animated with gorgeous imagery and huge fight scenes that were clearly crafted for the big screen… which is why it’s such a crime that this is being relegated to streaming on Paramount+.

There is a constant argument about whether movie theaters are the real deal while TV is some sort of second tier where movies get dumped once cinemas are done with them. But the quality and size of TVs and sound systems, the availability of the movies, and the erosion of the theatrical experience have put them pretty much on even footing, not to mention that the expense of movies makes it prohibitive to go to theaters for most viewers in the current economy even when you’re being hit in the face by popcorn while screaming teens yell “chicken jockey.” That’s not the argument we’re making here.

Instead, it’s clear from the opening frame of Avatar Aang to the closing scene that director Lauren Montgomery and company created this movie to be seen on a large theater screen. The original cartoon often depicted the beautiful open vistas of the world that Aang and friends fought to save, and in particular the final fight between Aang and Fire Lord Ozai is epic in scale. However, the widening of the frame throughout the new theatrical feature was clearly animated to be seen on the biggest screen possible; not your laptop, or your phone, and definitely not leaked on Twitter.

It’s an extremely unfortunate series of events that led to this slipshod release, from multiple shifting release dates, to Paramount changing their minds from a theatrical release to a streaming original, to the deplorable leak of the movie online back in April. And Avatar Aang doesn’t deserve any of this because on a whole, the movie is a lovingly crafted return to the world created by Michael Dante DiMartino and Bryan Konietzko, full of thrilling action and emotional moments alongside the aforementioned gorgeous animation and epic fight scenes. After an abysmal live-action movie and a not-that-great live-action TV adaptation, this should have been the rousing return to Avatar that fans have been asking for, and a chance for those fans to gather in theaters to cheer on the continuing adventures of Team Avatar. Instead, it’s marked with several weird asterisks.

That’s a lot of wind-up to get to the actual movie, but the table-setting is important, because the story around the movie is sadly more a part of the conversation than the content of the movie itself. Picking up a few years after the end of the animated series, Aang (Eric Nam) is now a teen/in his twenties, but still finding his way as the commander of the four elements — earth, air, fire, and water. He’s established Republic City, a place for “benders” — those who control one of those elements — to live together, with the caveat that he is the one and only airbender. While it’s hard to imagine someone with zero experience with Avatar coming into this clean, the movie does a nice job of recapping the original series both through the actions of Aang, and a revised opening sequence that shows off not just the magic inherent in the world, but also reintroduces the rest of Team Avatar and their current status quos.

The plot, per the title, focuses squarely on Aang as the newfound peace of the world is plagued by a new group called The Denied, non-benders who think the imbalance of power should be evened out. There’s also the surprise arrival of another, ancient airbender named Tagah (Dave Bautista). Thanks to Tagah and The Denied, Aang reassambles Team Avatar in a race across the world to capture a powerful artifact that may bring airbenders back or potentially fracture the balance established at the end of the original series. It’s vaguely reminiscent of 1987’s The Chipmunk Adventure, another hot air balloon race around the world, though with far fewer songs than that particular landmark animated feature.

The script by Tim Hedrick and Christopher Yost — the former was a veteran staff writer from the original series while the other is a staple of Marvel’s live-action and animated fare as well as comics — does a nice job of focusing on Aang and pushing him to a place where he needs to wrestle with his legacy in causing the problems that led to the triumph of the Fire Nation in the original series, while holding on to the fragile peace he gained at the end. Nam does a stellar job of voicing Aang, which was one of the big worries going into this movie. One joy of the original series was watching the tiny, young Aang wrestle with problems enormously larger than himself, physically and emotionally; thankfully, aging up his voice and body don’t take too much away from that.

A not inconsequential note here: The voice actors were almost entirely recast for the movie due to changing discussions about ethnic fidelity that have cropped up since the series initially aired. It’s laudable that DiMartino and Konietzko made this choice, and the new cast mostly — particularly given the rest of Team Avatar are in support mode and not much of the focus — works out just fine. Jessica Matten is the weakest as Aang’s love interest/waterbender Katara and over-pronounces every word, with Román Zaragoza as Katara’s brother Sokka and Dionne Quan as earthbender Toph ably aping the previous voice actors’ performances. The only one who truly makes the role his own is Invincible’s Steven Yeun as Fire Lord Zuko, who is actively hilarious when he needs to be and commanding when he’s not.

Bautista also makes a solid impression as Tagah, bringing some strong voice work and emotion to the ancient airbender, while Ken Jeong slots in just fine as the series’ cabbage merchant, still never getting a break. Geraldine Viswanathan also does good work as Kallak, a member of The Denied who is more sympathetic to the Avatar’s cause. On the other hand, there’s Taika Waititi, who is actively annoying and out of place in a shoved-in scene set in the spirit world seemingly designed only to allow for a celebrity guest appearance. The lone cast member ported from show to movie is Dee Bradley Baker as Appa and Momo, the flying bison and lemur, and Baker – a consummate pro – doesn’t miss a step.

While the visuals are stunning and the action intense, there’s not a lot to speak of on the story side — as indicated above — other than Aang’s arc. There’s really not much of a reason for the rest of Team Avatar to be there other than that it’s nice to see them again, and the plot twists (such as they are) are telegraphed from a mile away… not that the original The Last Airbender was a shock a minute by any means, but it often did go in surprising directions that expanded the world.

Avatar Aang functions more like the graphic novels from Dark Horse Comics, which slot nicely into story gaps rather than reinventing the mythology.

Avatar Aang functions more like the (usually excellent) graphic novels from Dark Horse Comics, which slot nicely into story gaps rather than reinventing the mythology. That said, the movie also laudably skirts the prequelitis that infects these sorts of entries. And while there are some neat easter eggs and explanations for plot points in sequel series The Legend of Korra, Avatar Aang never feels like it's holding your hand and walking you up to the events of Korra; those connections are merely a nice bonus.

Since Avatar Aang isn’t really trying to push the franchise forward in wild ways — for that we’ll have to wait for the post-apocalyptic Seven Havens TV series coming next year — it’s again supremely unfortunate that we were robbed of the way this would have been a special event: watching in a theater full of fellow screaming fans. It’s still surprisingly emotional to see these characters back on the small screen they called their home for so many years, and the movie is good enough that it functions as a nice epilogue while not detracting from the original in any way. But this could have been bigger and better, and all it needed to get there was Paramount’s confidence. Like Aang, the power is there; Paramount just needs to figure out how to use it for good, because sticking Avatar Aang next to Landman and Big Brother does the ambition of this lovingly crafted movie a massive disservice.

items.title: President Curtis Season 1 Review: It's No Rick and Morty
items.author: Jesse Schedeen
items.content:
This is a spoiler-free review of the first season of President Curtis. The first episode debuts on Adult Swim on July 26 and the following day on HBO Max.

For a series that takes place in a multiverse of infinite possibilities, Rick and Morty doesn’t have the greatest track record when it comes to spinoffs. 2022’s Vindicators 2 was a fine but forgettable short-form web series that never received a second season. 2024’s Rick and Morty: The Anime debuted to middling reviews, with IGN’s Allegra Frank writing, “The slow pacing and overall humorlessness may be hard for more casual Rick and Morty fans to stomach for 10 full episodes.” And now we have , a spinoff that also struggles in its own ways to capture the magic of its parent series. Your enjoyment of the show will depend entirely on how much affection you have for Keith David’s character.

And, to be fair, there is definitely some appeal to be found there. David’s President Andre Curtis has always been one of the more amusing of the many recurring players on Rick and Morty. As one of the characters in this series puts it, he’s a politician who thinks he’s Batman. There’s potential in a show centered around a POTUS who’s too busy beating up Paul Bunyan and staking angry vampires to be bothered with the humdrum political day-to-day of his job. The idea with President Curtis is that the series explores the same kinds of zany antics that Rick and Morty get into on a weekly basis, only seen through the lens of a straitlaced, hotheaded, ultimately well-meaning action hero.

That’s exactly what President Curtis delivers in its first season, no more and no less. Created by Rick and Morty veterans Dan Harmon and James Siciliano, the series is basically about Curtis battling the sort of terrestrial problems and threats that a man like Rick Sanchez couldn’t be bothered with. Each episode introduces a new cryptid or paranormal entity that only a no-nonsense President with an armory of fancy gadgets can handle. The series also establishes a new supporting cast of White House staffers, most notably Curtis’ bodyguard, Special Agent O’Doyle (Jim Rash), and his cybernetic Chief of Staff, Banks (Stephanie Beatriz).

The resulting formula is entertaining enough. Curtis remains a very likable hero, one who’s far more idealistic and well-meaning than we’re used to from this franchise. If you’ve ever found yourself wishing that the high-concept storytelling of Rick and Morty could be tempered by 70% less cynicism and nihilism, President Curtis is your show.

It doesn’t hurt that the show’s voice cast is so impressive. David, naturally, is the real highlight in that regard. His rugged yet velvety voice always makes any given project shine that little bit brighter. The writers clearly recognize as much, as most episodes end with a fun little stinger of Curtis recanting the episode’s events by singing in the shower. But Rash and Beatriz both deliver fantastic performances in their own right. Rash’s gung-ho, macho yet stoic O’Doyle is about as far removed from Community’s Dean as you can get, yet he’s still very effective in the role. And with Beatriz, it never ceases to amaze that her bubbly, personable voice is the same one that gave us Rosa Diaz on Brooklyn Nine-Nine.

President Curtis establishes a decent formula right out of the gate. Its problem is that it never really builds on that formula or finds ways to escape the shadow of Rick and Morty. There’s a same-y quality to these episodes that builds over time (as such, I wouldn’t recommend binging the whole season in one go). It’s not as if Rick and Morty has a perfect track record, but there’s enough variety in terms of tone and subject matter from episode to episode that you never quite know what you’re going to get with each new installment. That’s not really the case with President Curtis. The show feels inherently more limited in scope.

It would help if the series offered something to offset this problem. Why not a more character- or plot-driven alternative to Rick and Morty? This spinoff is a bit more interested in building an ongoing narrative, but only slightly. There’s some emphasis on the conflict between Curtis and his political party and his struggle to appeal to fickle voters, but that only comes into play in a couple of episodes. For the most part, it remains fixated on Curtis’ weekly antics and the minor conflicts he experiences with O’Doyle and Banks.

There’s just not a lot of depth to be found here. By the end of the season, I didn’t feel like I had any better a handle on Curtis as a character than I did from his original Rick and Morty appearances. He doesn’t grow or evolve. He doesn’t benefit from those stark moments of emotional insight like we occasionally get for Rick Sanchez and his family. His minions do fare somewhat better in that regard, at least.

Ultimately, President Curtis is an entertaining but generally one-note series that feels like it could be something more. It’ll probably scratch that itch for Rick and Morty fans who just want more of this universe . But as far as truly finding its own identity or making the most of its title character, President Curtis still has a lot of growing to do. We’ll see if it beats the Rick and Morty spinoff trend and actually gets a chance at a Season 2.

items.title: Rick and Morty Season 9, Episode 9 Review & Recap: "Salute Your Morts"
items.author: Jesse Schedeen
items.content:
Warning: This review contains full spoilers for Rick and Morty Season 9, Episode 9!

I think most of us would agree that Rick and Morty Season 9 was way overdue for a Beth-centric episode. After being relegated to the background for eight episodes, things finally change in the penultimate chapter of Season 9. "Salute Your Morts" is an overall fun romp, but it really excels when it comes to exploring how Beth (Sarah Chalke) and Jerry (Chris Parnell) deal with the curse of eternal intoxication.

"Salute Your Morts" benefits from a solid and very sensible premise. It only makes sense that all this exposure to advanced tech and multiversal capers and a grandfather who can bail them out of literally anything would transform Summer (Spencer Grammer) and Morty (Harry Belden) into entitled, elitist little brats. And of course, Rick's (Ian Cardoni) solution to that problem is to ship them off to an intergalactic afluenza camp.

That leads to an enjoyable dual-pronged storyline, as Summer and Morty experience a camp where the inmates are running the asylum and their parents enjoy their newfound freedom back home. Though, again, the Beth/Jerry storyline definitely overshadows the Summer/Morty one to some degree. It's a lot of fun watching these two stuffy dorks unwind and then find themselves lost in the never-ending high.

That storyline only grows more wacky as the two venture into space via Rick's intergalactic Uber device and travel to a world that's just one massive shopping mall. None of this would hit the same if it were Rick, Morty, or Summer dealing with the struggle to find Space Narcan. Beth and Jerry as the protagonists adds an extra level of novelty and silliness to the whole ordeal. It helps that Chalke and Parnell really go all-out with their vocal performances here.

It also helps that this episode uses Rick so sparingly and effectively. Rick being shuffled off to the side for a while allows Beth and Jerry to shine on their own before they finally cave and call him for a rescue. Then it turns out that Rick is just as incapacitated as they are, which leads to still more hilarity. The little subplot with Rick transforming people into oversized carrots is also a nice bonus, particularly with that excellent stinger scene at the very end.

None of this is to say that the Summer/Morty storyline doesn't have its share of laughs. You have to love the Teenage Mutant Ninja Turtles movie reference when the two are being introduced to their new, hedonistic playground. And it's certainly fun to watch as that playground rapidly devolves into a waking nightmare. I do think this storyline loses a bit of steam as it goes along, with the whole "townies vs. rich camp kids" development feeling a bit halfhearted. But still, it's impossible not to be entertained by watching a drugged-out Rick going to town on their wouldbe kidnappers. It's a rare episode where the entire Smith/Sanchez clan is allowed to shine.

items.title: House of the Dragon Season 3, Episode 5 Recap and Review
items.author: Jim Vejvoda
items.content:
Is Rhaenyra paranoid, or is someone really out to get her? Well, definitely the latter – but she might also be paranoid, and that’s the problem. There is plotting on all sides: some relatively benign, and some potentially lethal, and it’s hard to tell where the real dangers are coming from. Things continue tense, in other words, and that is keeping this show as gripping as Game Of Thrones ever was.

We open on a frustrated Lord Oscar Tully (Archie Barnes), whose attempts to get his Rivermen army to Tumbleton have been stymied by Christen Cole (Fabian Frankel) and his guerilla warriors burning bridges along the route. Cole attacks the Rivermen camp that night, but that’s as good as it gets for him. The Northmen soon get ahead of him and lay caltrops on the road to Stony Sept, the next river crossing, forcing Cole, Gwayne Hightower (Freddie Fox) and company off their horses and into woods and bogs, through rain and leeches.

Gwayne gives up at the end, but Cole is still determined to make a mark for himself. “I will not let them diminish me now…I am the realm’s greatest warrior, and I will die as such, clear-eyed, unbent, glorious in battle, and not in their service, finally, but in my own. And they will sing songs of it, and Queen Alicent herself will hear them.” Somehow this particular display of arrogance makes him more likeable and not less; after spending all of last season moping, this guy is fascinating by comparison.

Alys (Gayle Rankin) is still claiming that she knows nothing about Aemond (Ewan Mitchell) to the various bounty hunters that turn up at Harrenhal, but upstairs she’s busy nursing him back to health and also giving the awful little monster some of the affection he craves. There’s an interesting thread here where Aemond is tormented by visions of his brother Aegon (Tom Glynn-Carney) dying and convinced his family doesn’t love , while Aegon harps on about the wrongs done to him by his brother and is convinced the family doesn’t love . Alys comforts Aemond while Larys (Matthew Needham) calls Aegon out on his bullshit – arguably the right response in both cases – but it’s not clear if it will be enough to force either to change his ways.

At least Aegon and Larys are reunited with Tyland Lannister (Jefferson Hall), while Aegon kills his tormentor Janos (Oliver Coopersmith) in order to cover up his identity (and for vengeance). It’s a kind of return to form after his recent helplessness, even if “form” in this case means “bloody viciousness”. Aemond, too, is out of his sick bed and swinging a sword about, so prepare for these two to cause trouble again soon.

"Things are tougher for Queen Rhaenyra, still hounded on all sides. 

This is also a really interesting episode for their sister, and Aegon’s wife, Helaena (Phia Saban). She refuses to drink the abortifacient her mother procures from Grand Maester Orwyle (Kurt Egyiawan) because she won’t give up her baby despite all of Alicent’s political realism. She speaks confidently of Aemond’s mysterious whereabouts, and fights hard to escape the Red Keep – but if she can bring her little daughter Jaehaera and the babe in utero. Luckily for her the devil works hard, but Alicent works harder to keep her daughter alive, sometimes in spite of Helaena’s own wishes. By the end of the episode Alicent has downed the potion herself, given up the symbol of her faith to facilitate their escape, convinced Mysaria (Sonoya Mizuno) to keep her secrets, and is trapped in a secret passage inside the castle trying to flee.

Things are tougher for Queen Rhaenyra, still hounded on all sides. Lady Frey (Sarah Woodward) has come to King’s Landing to join her council and to harangue her for promising Harrenhal elsewhere. Addam (Clinton Liberty) and Baela (Bethany Antonia) are out searching for Vhagar and, at least on Addam’s part, flirting madly in an attempt to cheer her up (she’s still feeling guilt over Jace’s death at the Gullet).

In the council, there’s chaos. Mysaria speaks for the people; Daemon for the Gold Cloaks police force; Rhaenyra focuses only on Aemond. Everyone is at cross-purposes. Rhaenyra tries to rein Daemon in, but he points out that there’s no rush to find Vhagar and that there are other priorities. After a theological debate in the High Sept, their dispute becomes clearer: she thinks the gods made her queen so can’t understand why they now seem to abandon her; he is baffled by this fatalistic faith. However, he has his own faith: a speech to the Gold Cloaks about turning the Seven Kingdoms into “Nine Kingdoms, Twelve, the new Valyria” feels like a glimpse into Daemon’s belief system.

Still, Rhaenyra is ungrounded and losing perspective; she can’t bring herself to spend time with her younger son, Joffrey, and is convinced that there’s a dark power out to get her. Her tunnel vision is becoming a problem; she lacks the flexibility of mind to see problems beyond her own status, her own family. That is thrown into sharp focus by the episode’s finale, where Daemon – who has kept the Gold Cloaks on side with stirring speeches and promises of double pay one day in future – returns to their headquarters to find a massacre, the bodies posed in a grotesque tableau.

Behind it all, Lord Ormund Hightower (James Norton) is less of a focus this episode, but has one scene to make his presence felt. He’s getting messages from the assassins of the Gold Cloaks, and still terrorising / mentoring his nephew Daeron (Benjamin Evan Ainsworth), this time with tales of the military leadership of Meria Martell, the “Yellow Toad” who defied Aegon I and his Conquest of Westeros. He’s so distracted by his history lesson that he loses their game of cyvasse (aka not chess), terrifying Daeron.

But Ormund is merciful, too pleased with his shadow war and the coins he’s had minted with Daeron’s face on them, the ones he’s using to pay his assassins, to lose his temper this time. Everything is still on track for him, if only he could find Vhagar. The giant dragon feels like the wild card that everyone is waiting for, the one element holding them all in place. Let’s hope we see more of him than a shadow and a footprint next time.

items.title: King of the Hill Season 15 Review
items.author: Michael Peyton
items.content:
King of the Hill Season 15 will be released on July 20 on Hulu and Disney+.

If the 13 original seasons of King of the Hill were great and last year’s 10 new episodes were even better, Season 15 of the animated masterpiece is something else entirely: a rare season of television that is somehow better than everything that came before it.

When King of the Hill returned in 2025 after a 16-year hiatus, it was a spectacular, if not jarring, homecoming for one of the greatest animated series of all time. The characters that we had come to love over 13 seasons were suddenly back in our lives, aged up and facing new challenges big and small. They were the same Hank, Peggy, Bobby, Dale, Nancy, Boomhauer, Connie, Minh, Khan, and Bill that we’d always known but they, like the rest, of us had evolved.

Hank was semi-retired after he and Peggy spent years building a nest egg in Saudi Arabia. Bobby was the head chef at a Japanese-German fusion restaurant in Dallas. Connie was in college. Dale had served a stint as mayor of Arlen. Bill, well, he was still Bill.

As that season progressed we moved past the excitement the new episodes provided and slowly sank into the warm bath of the King of the Hill we’d always loved, even if the characters had changed and grown. If Season 14 was slightly burdened by being novel (Hank and Peggy moved to Saudi Arabia! Look at how much older Bobby is! Will we get answers about what happened to Lucky and Luanne?), Season 15 is completely unshackled from any need to explain, indulge, or prove anything to anyone, and stands as one of the most expertly crafted and profound seasons of television of the past 20 years.

The show walks a tightrope of being one of the few animated series that actually allows its characters to change and grow while still feeding its audience a steady diet of what truly makes the series great -- a beautiful, steady rhythm of slice-of-life stories and familiar characters that resonate episode after episode.

In true King of the Hill fashion, every episode of Season 15 centers around a storyline that is instantly relatable: Hank is annoyed that someone keeps parking their electric truck in front of his house; Hank is frustrated that the toothpaste is locked up at Mega Lo Mart; Bobbie and Connie decide to “hard launch” their relationship; Hank and Peggy get scammed; Dale raises chickens; Hank and Bobby visit a fan convention; Peggy experiences menopause; etc. Yet nothing ever feels tired or dull. The beautiful writing and expert voice acting are always hand-in-glove, so realistic and relatable that you often forget that you’re watching a cartoon.

Mike Judge (Hank, Boomhauer) and Kathy Najimy (Peggy) are still at the top of their game after all this time. They deftly lean into their characters’ golden years and breathe life into every “yep” and “ho-yeah!” There’s nothing particularly new that they’re doing here, but their continued grasp of what are easily career-defining characters after nearly three decades is a testament to their incredible skill. Similarly, Pamela Adlon (Bobby), Stephen Root (Bill), Ashley Gardner (Nancy), Lauren Tom (Minh, Connie), and the rest of the cast continue to shine. Adlon and Tom in particular excel in aging up their two main characters in a nuanced way. Thanks in no small part to their performances, Season 15 lets us grow more comfortable with the grown-up versions of Connie and Bobby as Tom and Adlon transform them into distinct yet familiar versions of the characters we love.

Similarly Toby Huss, who took over voicing Dale Gribble from Johnny Hardwick, who passed away in 2023, has fully come into his own as the lead voice for one of the show’s most iconic characters. In Season 14, Huss shared the role with Hardwick, but this season it's fully his. I’ll admit that it was originally a bit of a jolt to hear someone new voice Dale last year. But over the course of Season 15, Huss flawlessly performs the difficult task of paying homage to Hardwick’s creation while making Dale fully his own.

Outside of the core group of characters, Season 15 is full of fan-favorites who we haven’t seen in a while. Hank’s Strickland Propane colleagues Joe Jack, Booda Sack, and Enrique come to Hank in a bind late in the season. Hank’s propane nemesis M.F. Thatherton makes an appearance. Reverend Stroup shows up for an episode. Even ne'er-do-well Jimmy Wichard makes a cameo.

Beyond returning characters, the new episodes are jam-packed with Easter eggs and callbacks to the show’s original run. King of the Hill Season 15 threads the needle of giving diehard fans nearly everything they could possibly ask for while never digging too deeply into gimmicks or fan service. For example, we see the return of Bobby’s ventriloquist dummy, Chip Block, a character tries out to be a sports mascot, Bill joins another cult-like organization, and the list goes on.

But none of these callbacks drag the show back into the “glory days” or lean too heavily into sentimentality. They’re just lovely moments in a well-executed show that acknowledge the love that fans have for what the cast and crew have created. And therein lies the magic trick of King of the Hill: It knows exactly how to let their characters learn, grow, and progress through life while never shying away from what made the show great to begin with.

The series is not the same as when it premiered or even when it first went off the air. In Season 15, Hank and Peggy walk in on Bobby and Connie . There’s a plethora of wonderful, appropriate and hilarious cursing (at one point, Peggy exclaims “Shit on a shingle!"). In another episode, characters gleefully consume what, in the '90s, would have been considered an illicit drug. But King of the Hill is still the show we’ve always loved. There’s still heart, warmth, and plenty of propane.

Never is that more evident than in the season finale. Dating back to the beginning of last season, the one minor nitpick I had with the King of the Hill reboot was that it thrust us right back into the characters' lives without explaining much about what had happened in the intervening years. Episode 10 of Season 15, titled “Propane Recall,” sees Hank’s former colleagues pull him back into the propane game to help save their flailing business.

What follows is a near-episode-long flashback showing a good chunk of what happened during the eight years of screen time between Seasons 13 and 14. Revisiting Hank, Peggy, and the gang as they were years ago is heartwarming, even if 16-year-old Bobby had a bit of a punk phase (blue hair and all). We get numerous answers about why Hank took a job in Saudi Arabia, why Bobby became a chef, and more. There’s even a brief mention of Luanne and Lucky (voiced by the late Brittany Murphy and Tom Petty, respectively), which I won’t spoil here, but it ties together a few loose ends and serves as a simultaneous gut punch and touching moment. The whole sequence is a reminder of why we loved the show during its original run and why we still love it now.

Season 15 of King of the Hill is as perfect a season of television as you could hope for. The team behind the show has taken a beautiful prime cut of story, coated it in top-tier vocal performances, and expertly grilled it on an open (clean-burning) flame of nostalgia, heart, and warmth. For fans of the series, or just good storytelling in general, it’s lightyears beyond whatever you could ever possibly want from a new batch of episodes. And given the show’s history, that’s no small task, I tell you what.

items.title: Cape Fear Episode 8 Recap and Review – ‘Los tiempos de Dios son Perfectos’
items.author: Michael Peyton
items.content:
Spoilers below for Episode 8 of . New episodes stream every Friday on Apple TV.

As Cape Fear barrels toward its inevitable explosive conclusion, I find myself queuing up each new episode with the same thought: This one can’t be as flat-out nuts as the last one, can it? And every time I get the same answer: Yes, it absolutely can.

This week’s episode, titled “Los tiempos de Dios son Perfects” (Spanish for “God’s timing is perfect”), is so surprising, so melodramatic, and so propulsive that it left me in a state akin to Jack Torrance’s “all work and no play makes Jack a dull boy” moment in The Shining in which I repeatedly wrote “BONKERS” in all caps on my computer. Because this episode, filled with floating body parts, double crosses, and revelations is BONKERS.

Episode 8 picks up moments after where , when Tom and Anna tried to frame Max by convincing Anna’s father, Brandon, to plant suspicious medication in Max’s house. Soon, the police arrive and take Max into custody. Max glares at Tom and Anna while he’s loaded into a police car, and the look on Natalie’s face signals that she suspects something’s up as well. Meanwhile, back at Zack’s psychiatric facility, the doctor thinks he has Zack on the road to recovery. But, this being Cape Fear, things aren’t that cut and dry. Zack gets angry and begins spiraling, signaling that he might still be under Max’s control.

The next morning, Natalie goes for a swim in the back yard, leading to what is perhaps the most jarring and grotesque scene in the entire series. While swimming laps, Natalie spots a box at the bottom of the pool. What could possibly be inside? Is it the missing gun? Evidence of Anna and Tom’s plot for framing Max? No. Natalie swims to it and pops open the box only to see body parts come spewing out. Lots and lots of body parts. Ray’s, to be specific. Natalie recoils in horror as the scene cuts to the best title sequence to date.

What makes this scene special — my second favorite of the series so far, after — is not only the shock and gore but the fact that it finally, conclusively shows that Max is 100% behind all of the Bowdens’ misery. We always knew this (the show is titled Cape Fear, after all, and its main character is Max Cady) but it’s a relief to finally have Max’s treachery laid out explicitly. The revelation frees the show from some of its long-running questions and hopefully portends an action-packed finale.

After the Bowdens call the police, Natalie comes to the realization that Max used the gun she stole from her parents to kill Ray. When the cops ask Tom if he owns any guns, he goes to the safe and realizes that one has been fired. So what does Tom do? Surely, as a lawyer, something sensible, right? Nope. Once Tom realizes something’s off, he decides to give the cops a different gun.

After confronting Max in the street after he’s released from custody (why do so many tense moments in this show happen in the middle of the street?), Anna goes to see her father. She tells him to get out of town so the cops don’t tie him to the plot to blackmail Max. Brandon, ever the loving father, agrees as long as Anna pays him $100,000.

Next, after talking to Max on the phone, Natalie goes to see Neveah in jail and begs for her help. Neveah instead screams at Natalie to the point where she has to be escorted away by guards. Later, Anna and Noa use iCloud to access photos Ray took on his trip to North Carolina and find pictures of the houseboat. Tom and Anna show the photos to the police, who are dismissive. They question Tom again about the gun and tell them they know he gave them the wrong one.

At home, Natalie comes clean with her parents about the gun and her trip to North Carolina just as the police arrive and arrest Tom for killing Ray, who they think discovered a plot for Tom to frame Max. Anna sees Ray on the news with Max and realizes that her father was the one who betrayed them. Anna visits Tom in prison who asks her if Max is Natalie’s daughter. She obfuscates and says the situation is complicated.

All of this is essential to the plot if frustating; setting up what looks like will be another delay where we will be denied real answers to one of the show’s biggest questions. I was afraid the show was slipping back into its early season habit of spinning its wheels and putting off the inevitable good stuff but, fortunately, was immediately proved wrong.

Just as we’re left wondering when we’ll (if ever) find out about the truth of Anna and Max’s prior relationship, we’re given an answer in the very next scene. Natalie asks Anna point blank: “What did Max Cady do to you?” In an eerie analog to Zack’s missing memories, Anna reveals that she experienced blackouts back when she was Max’s attorney, sometimes for a few hours, sometimes for whole nights, and even entire weekends. Anna says she was a “disaster” at the time and didn’t really suspect anything because it had “happened before” with other men. She claims not to know who Natalie’s father is, but what’s certain is that Max Cady’s machinations are even more evil than we suspected.

At Max’s house, he experiences another seizure and has a vision of his dead wife and son before Natalie comes bursting in with a gun and tries to get him to “tell the truth” about what he did to her mother. Thinking he’s diffused the situation, Max pulls Natalie close before she pulls the trigger and shoots him in the stomach. Soon, the police show up for the third time this episode and take Natalie into custody. As she’s loaded into a police car, Anna runs out and asks Natalie what happened. “I missed,” she replies.

At the hospital, Anna pretends to be Max’s sister Crystal to confront him once again. When she asks what he wants from her, Max – who miraculously survived, thanks to the bullet missing all of his major organs – lays out his fundamental motive: “ is what I wanted. For you to feel this. To feel your family ripped apart. Broken. Caged. Your children taken away. This.”

Enraged, Anna storms out and returns home where she’s able to pull up location data from one of the iCloud photos. She grabs a gun and hits the road while a literal hurricane bears down. She’s headed to the Cape Fear River to find Crystal. God knows what happens next. The sequence showcases Amy Adams’s best acting of the entire series, effortlessly pivoting between despair, confusion, and rage as she steamrolls her way into an all-out war with Max Cady.

As the episode ends we’re left with glimpses of just how dire Anna’s situation is. Her husband? Jail. Her daughter? Jail. Her son? Involuntary psychiatric hold. “Los tiempos de Dios son Perfectos” is simultaneously the culmination of a few long-simmering storylines and the launching pad for the show’s endgame. While it’s not as action-packed or humorous as some previous entries, Episode 8 of Cape Fear is a rollicking hour filled with outright horror, long-awaited answers, and the best acting of the show so far.

items.title: The Hawk Review
items.author: Arnold T. Blumberg
items.content:
The Hawk debuts on Netflix on July 16.

It can be uncomfortable seeing someone that you know can easily be funny as hell just totally misfire with a new comic character, but hey — it truly happens to the best of them. If it’s just one comedy sketch among many, you can just move on to the next one, but if it’s a movie, that’s more difficult because there’s more of a time investment. Still, after 90 minutes or so, the movie wraps up and you can still be hopeful for the next time out.

Ah, but if it’s a ten-episode TV show, and neither the central character nor the show around them is ever really coming together and rarely delivering laughs, it’s a much more frustrating experience to say the least. And sadly, such is the case with The Hawk, the new Netflix series starring Will Ferrell.

Mugatu, Buddy the Elf, Ron Burgundy, Ricky Bobby, Brennan Huff: The list of standout Will Ferrell characters is a long one, and I’m only pulling from his movies with that list and not even including the huge amount of awesome, memorable Saturday Night Live characters and sketches he gave us. After he left SNL and became a genuine movie star, beyond some guest star roles, Ferrell’s dabbled back in TV here and there with projects like The Spoils of Babylon. The Hawk, however, marks his biggest foray into playing a continuing character so far; he’s Lonnie "The Hawk" Hawkins, a past-his-prime golfer attempting a comeback.

Lonnie has all of the trappings of several classic Will Ferrell characters in terms of his man-child vibes, his unearned off-the-charts self-confidence, and his penchant for accidentally hurting himself. And yet all of these traits never come together in a satisfying manner; Lonnie always feels like a patchy of a comedy character rather than one who’s actually fully formed, but since he’s at the center of the series, he’s inescapable and increasingly annoying.

This awkward vibe is there from the start with an early scene where Lonnie plays a golf game to the tune of “The Thong Song,” singing and dancing along and then revealing he’s wearing a thong himself. You can see why the idea of Will Ferrell wearing a thong be funny, and yet this is just funny, and isn’t really motivated by anything occurring nor really ever returned to again as far as who Lonnie is. It’s one of many scenes where you can’t help but think of great Ferrell bits from the past being semi-echoed, like that SNL sketch where he wore the tiny red, white and blue shorts to show his post-9/11 patriotism, and how hysterical that was vs. this just not landing at all.

What’s notable is that Ferrell is surrounded on The Hawk by many other skilled and usually very funny people too, all to no avail. The series was co-created by Ferrell and two longtime collaborators, Harper Steele and Chris Henchy, with Steele in particular standing out for writing so many awesome SNL sketches, plenty of which starred Ferrell. Two of Ferrell’s lauded SNL co-stars, Molly Shannon and Chris Parnell, are among the cast, as is his Old School co-star, Luke Wilson. Andrew Guest, fresh off the triumph of serving as showrunner on the excellent Wonder Man, is among the writers. So much talent has assembled here for something so flat.

What went wrong? It’s hard to say. The Hawk was originally going to be a series co-created by Ferrell, Ramy Youssef, and Josh Rabinowitz, with Youssef intended to star alongside Ferrell. That sounds like a really interesting blending of sensibilities, and perhaps once Youssef and Rabinowitz departed the series — over the requisite "creative differences” — so did the show’s actual inspiration for existing, with this remaining as the hollow leftovers. Or maybe it’s just the dreaded Netflix curse that so often occurs when proven stars make some of their most career-mediocre to career-worst films and series for the streaming service (there are of course some genuinely good or outright terrific exceptions).

Whatever the reason, more often than not, the ten episodes of The Hawk are just meandering and dull. With this many legitimately funny people involved, there are some genuine big laughs to be found at times throughout the series, and a really clever line or an amusingly surreal imaginary sequence will pop in now and again, but they are way too few and far between.

More often than not, the ten episodes of The Hawk are just meandering and dull.

The center of the series is Lonnie’s struggle to reconnect with his son, Lance (Jimmy Tatro), who’s followed in his footsteps and become a skilled pro golfer in his own right. But the push-pull between these characters — who, inevitably, find themselves facing off on the golf course — is neither amusing nor engaging enough to feel worth investing in. It doesn’t help that Tatro, who’s been a very witty scene stealer in projects like American Vandal and Theater Camp, is put in the straight man role for much of The Hawk’s running time. Aside from some Vegas-set bits later in the season, he’s mostly asked to play annoyed and exasperated in reaction to Ferrell rather than given the opportunity to try to create many laughs himself.

An absolute comedic pro like Molly Shannon does all she can to elicit laughs as Lonnie’s estranged wife, occasionally connecting through sheer force of will, yet she still struggles thanks to being given a character whose main trait initially is “curses a lot.” Various subplots burble in and out, including a potential love triangle for Lance, or Lonnie’s caddy (Fortune Feimster) reconnecting with her own dad, but little of it comes to life or feels worthwhile.

That being said, kudos to Patty Guggenheim, who made such a big impression as She-Hulk: Attorney at Law’s transcendent Madisynn. King has a relatively small amount of screen time compared to others in the cast, playing the love interest to Parnell’s mega-jerk character, but still manages to make the most of it, bringing some decent wry humor into a show that desperately could use more of it.

items.title: The Odyssey Review
items.author: Scott Collura
items.content:
The Odyssey opens in theaters on July 17.

“Couldn’t you have shown some mercy?”

That line is the crux of Christopher Nolan’s much-anticipated adaptation of , a film that is truly huge in scope while also at times emotionally acute. Using Homer’s foundational epic poem as a lens through which to offer a surprisingly modern lesson about war, PTSD, and the very question of we still have to deal with those things almost 3,000 years since The Odyssey first came into existence, Nolan’s opus is resonant and often bold. Unfortunately, it also occasionally misses a step in its storytelling, and frequently feels rushed despite its nearly three-hour running time. The result is a must-see if sometimes frustrating film that is marked with moments of brilliance.

Anyone who bothered to read at least the first few chapters of The Odyssey in 10th grade English class should know the story, but a quick refresher: Matt Damon stars as Odysseus, the king/warrior/husband/father who has been missing from his home island of Ithaca for some 20 years, flung across the realm of ancient, mythological Greece after being summoned by the king of the Achaean kings, Agamemnon (Benny Safdie). Told in nonchronological fashion (hey, , but also hey, Homer did it first), Odysseus’ wife, Penelope (Anne Hathaway), and their now-on-the-verge-of-manhood son, Telemachus (Tom Holland), suffer back at home, wondering if their king will ever return, even while the greedy and very un-cool suitors, led by Robert Pattinson’s Antinous, run roughshod over Odysseus’ home, vying for his wealth – and his wife.

It’s a story that has been told on film before, if never very successfully (2024's , starring Ralph Fiennes, is great, but it only depicts the last act of Homer's work). And Nolan, perhaps inevitably, makes tweaks to the original poem that might bug Homer enthusiasts, changing bits here, compressing story threads there, adding in elements from other Greek mythology works, and even occasionally removing some of the most iconic moments from the original (don’t hold your breath if you’re waiting for Odysseus to pull his “I am Nobody” trick on the Cyclops here). That said, there is no way to translate all of The Odyssey into a feature film without nipping and tucking and (in the spirit of the work) dismembering some elements. And besides, the folks who never made it past Chapter 3 in high school are not gonna notice anyway.

Among the most thrilling aspects of Nolan’s film are the horror elements that he brings to the highly episodic escapades of Odysseus and his men.

Among the most thrilling aspects of Nolan’s film are the horror elements that he brings to the highly episodic escapades of Odysseus and his men as they struggle to find their way back home after the Trojan War, only to fall on bad luck time and again. For example, the run-in with the aforementioned Cyclops (achieved via visual effect but the performance of which was “guided” by the great Bill Irwin) removes much of the dialogue between the one-eyed monster and Odysseus from the book, but in its place the director inserts a sort of existential dread as the thing goes about its day-to-day business of waking up, herding sheep, eating Odysseus’ men, and going to sleep, before doing it all over again the next day. The beast’s grotesque design and otherworldly sound design add to the creep factor, but the Cyclops also seems to exist on a sort of higher level than mere man, barely aware of the crew’s presence when not chomping on them. As Damon’s Odysseus notes, we don’t try to talk to ants, so why should this beast try to talk to us?

A trip to the Underworld is spectacularly realized, as Odysseus encounters, among others, the men who have died under his command. “I died for your lies,” says the Shade of Elliot Page’s Sinon (the actor is not playing Achilles here, despite fan speculation). And therein lies the rub for Odysseus, for how many have died because of his machinations over the past 20 years?

Another memorable/horrific moment comes when Samantha Morton’s witch Circe transforms Odysseus’ men into pigs, morphing them and sculpting them with her bare hands Rick Baker-style, but only after a gross-out meal of magic stew sets the stage. That Circe winds up coming across as sort of justified in her actions just serves to underline the film’s thematic examination of what war does to man… or is it more a question of why man does war?

But that’s also one of the problems at the core of Nolan’s The Odyssey, despite its strokes of beauty, ugliness, and ingenuity in re-telling Homer’s story. Damon plays the hero as a conflicted warrior whose invention, the Trojan Horse, won the war for Agamemnon. But to what end? After the war, the character is set adrift emotionally as much as he is literally lost at sea.

The issue with this notion, however, is that Odysseus arrives at this state of mind early in his story chronologically, and yet we see him continue to make the same kind of mistakes time and again that landed him there in the first place, as if he hasn’t learned his lesson despite his revelation. Additionally, while Damon is perfectly serviceable as Odysseus, he’s also lacking the mischievous, trickster edge which is inherent in the classic take on the character. In Greek mythology, Odysseus basically pulled a Klinger from M*A*S*H, trying to get out of having to go to war by acting like he was insane. But in the film, he dutifully and nobly heeds the call of Agamemnon. (Interestingly, it’s Pattinson’s Antinous who’s the draft dodger here.)

Speaking of which, the Batman actor does what he can with what is basically the main villain role, but the suitors are essentially just depicted as bad guys doing bad things. The nuance that some interpretations of The Odyssey have brought to the suitors – the idea, for example, that Odysseus drafted their fathers, grandfathers, and older brothers to go off and all die in the war, only for the king to be the sole survivor to return home – is not really explored by Nolan, unfortunately.

As for Tom Holland’s Telemachus, this is another character who can be frustrating to watch (or read), since frustrated as well by his inability to act against the villains who have overtaken his home. Holland doesn’t quite find a way around this dilemma, and even when the cathartic final battle with the suitors arrives – those guys are screwed – Telemachus doesn’t get much to do.
